title: Health informatics — Guidance on standards for enabling safety in health software
description: This document provides guidance to National Member Bodies and readers by identifying a coherent set of international standards relevant to the development, implementation and use of safer health software.
body:
Health informatics and associated eHealth systems have significant potential to eliminate, reduce or mitigate documented threats to patient safety and quality of care. But they can also introduce safety risks.
This document provides guidance to National Member Bodies and readers by identifying a coherent set of international standards relevant to the development, implementation and use of safer health software. It provides guidance on the applicability of these standards towards enabling optimal safety in health software within overall risk management and quality management approaches, as well as within the lifecycle steps and processes of health-software development. It also addresses the health-software safety issues that remain as gaps or overlaps between or among the identified standards and discusses how those gaps and overlaps could be addressed by revising current standards or developing new ones.
Included is a framework (with mapped standards) to illustrate relevant standards and how they can be optimally applied. The mapping serves to demonstrate where gaps and overlaps in standards exist.

· Scope
· Health software safety
· Standards assessment and guidance
· Annex A: Patient safety benefits arising from eHealth investments
· Annex B: Standards analysis from a software lifecycle perspective
· Annex C: Scope information of safety-relevant JTC 1 standards

title: Health informatics – System of concepts to support continuity of care
description: This document defines the generic concepts needed to achieve continuity of care, including both the content and context of healthcare services.
body:
Continuity of care is an important aspect of quality and safety in health care. Semantic interoperability is a basic requirement for continuity of care. This document defines the generic concepts needed to achieve continuity of care, including both the content and context of healthcare services.
Healthcare and clinical processes reflect the interaction between a subject of care and healthcare professionals. This standard is designed to establish a common conceptual framework across national, cultural and professional barriers. It is intended to be the foundation for interoperability at all levels in healthcare organisations and for development of information systems in healthcare.

title: Health informatics – Categorial structure for terminological systems of human anatomy
description: This document defines the characteristics required to describe the organisation and content of human anatomy within a terminological system.
body:
This document defines the characteristics required to describe the organisation and content of human anatomy within a terminological system. It is designed primarily for use with computer-based applications such as clinical electronic health records, in decision support and for various biomedical research purposes.
The standard is intended to facilitate the construction of new terminological systems in a regular form which will increase their coherence and expressiveness, increase the consistency and coherence of existing terminological systems and facilitate the convergence of human anatomy terms in terminological systems. It does not provide categorial structure for developmental anatomy (e.g. prenatal development, postnatal growth and ageing).
The intended audience includes designers of healthcare terminological categorial structures, developers of healthcare terminological systems and developers of information systems that require an explicit representation of healthcare terminological systems. It is not suitable for use by individual clinicians or hospital administrators.

title: Health informatics — Health indicators conceptual framework
description: This standard establishes a common health indicators conceptual framework, and aims to foster a common vocabulary and conceptual definitions for the framework. The framework defines the appropriate dimensions and sub-dimensions required to describe the health of the population and performance of a healthcare system.
body:
This standard establishes a common health indicators conceptual framework, and aims to foster a common vocabulary and conceptual definitions for the framework. The framework defines the appropriate dimensions and sub-dimensions required to describe the health of the population and performance of a healthcare system. It is sufficiently broad (high-level) to accommodate a variety of healthcare systems, and is comprehensive, encapsulating all of the factors related to health outcomes and health system performance and utilisation, as well as regional and national variations. The standard does not identify or describe individual indicators or specific data elements for the health indicators conceptual framework, nor does it address needs analysis, demand analysis or the range of activities that need to be supported for health system management. The definition of benchmarks and/or approaches used in the definition of benchmarks is outside the standard’s scope.

title: Health informatics — Capacity-based eHealth architecture roadmap – Part 2: Architectural components and maturity model
description: This document provides a guide to best-practice business requirements and principles relating to information and communications technology (ICT) for the delivery and development of healthcare. It applies to countries and their subordinate health authorities that are planning or implementing ICT to support healthcare.
body:
This document provides a guide to best-practice business requirements and principles relating to information and communications technology (ICT) for the delivery and development of healthcare. It applies to countries and their subordinate health authorities that are planning or implementing ICT to support healthcare.
A business reference architecture is described in terms of components and capabilities that health authorities may use as a framework for building their own eHealth architectures. This can also be used to measure the maturity of their health systems' use of ICT to support the delivery and development of healthcare.
This document also proposes a maturity model and methodology that organisations may consider in developing and evolving particular eHealth capacities. The development of eHealth architectures based on the guidelines set out in this document will facilitate and optimise investments in health information systems.

title: Health informatics — Personal health records — Definition, scope and context
description: This document gives a definition of a personal health record (PHR) to help clarify the types of records that should be called PHRs.
body:
Personal health records (PHRs) differ from electronic health records, and there is a lack of consistency in how the term PHR is applied. This document gives a definition of a PHR to help clarify the types of records that should be called PHRs. It considers the PHR from the perspective of the personal information contained within it and the core services needed to manage this information.
A PHR encompasses a spectrum of possible information repositories and services. This document discusses the scope of the PHR in terms of this spectrum as a series of dimensions by which a PHR may be classified and equivalent PHR products compared. It also includes one dimension to classify the types of collaborative-care-PHRs provided by healthcare organisations.
Scenarios for collaborative care between individuals and healthcare organisations are also included, to reflect the role of PHRs in the wider context of engaging individuals in managing their health.

· Scope
· Definition of a PHR
· Scope of the PHR
· Context of the PHR

· Annex B: Relationship of this technical report to the HL7 PHR System Functional Model

title: Health Informatics — Personal health device communication – Part 10472: Device specialization — Medication monitor
description: This standard establishes a normative definition of communication between personal telehealth medication monitor devices and compute engines in a way that enables plug-and-play interoperability.
body:
This standard establishes a normative definition of communication between personal telehealth medication monitor devices and compute engines (for example, cell phones, personal computers, personal health appliances and set-top boxes) in a way that enables plug-and-play interoperability.
This standard leverages appropriate portions of existing standards including ISO/IEEE 11073 terminology, information models, application profile standards, and transport standards. It specifies the use of specific term codes, formats, and behaviours in telehealth environments restricting optionality in base frameworks in favour of interoperability. A common core of communication functionality for personal telehealth medication monitor devices is also defined.
This standard has been superseded by ISO/IEEE 11073-10472:2024 Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 10472: Personal health device communication — Device specialization — Medication monitor.

title: Health informatics — Personal health device communication – Part 10471: Device specialization - Independent living activity hub
description: This standard establishes a normative definition of the communication between independent-living activity hubs and managers in a way that enables plug-and-play interoperability.
body:
This standard establishes a normative definition of the communication between independent-living activity hubs and managers (for example, cell phones, personal computers, personal health appliances and set-top boxes) in a way that enables plug-and-play interoperability. In this context, independent-living activity hubs are defined as devices that communicate with simple situation monitors (binary sensors), normalise information received from the monitor, and provide it to one or more managers. This information can be examined (for example) to determine when a person's activities or behaviours have deviated significantly from what is normal for them so that relevant parties can be notified.
This standard leverages appropriate portions of existing standards including ISO/IEEE 11073 terminology and information models. It specifies the use of specific term codes, formats, and behaviours in telehealth environments restricting ambiguity in base frameworks in favour of interoperability. A common core of communication functionality for independent-living activity hubs is also defined.
This standard has been superseded by ISO/IEEE 11073-10471:2024 Health informatics — Device interoperability – Part 10471: Personal health device communication — Device specialization — Independent living activity hub.

title: Health informatics — Individual case safety reports (ICSRs) in pharmacovigilance – Part 1: Framework for adverse event reporting
description: This standard provides a common messaging format for transmitting individual case safety reports for adverse drug reactions, adverse events, product problems and consumer complaints that can occur upon the administration or use of one or more products.
body:
This standard refers to individual case safety reports (ICSRs) for adverse drug reactions (ADR), adverse events (AE), product problems and consumer complaints that can occur from the administration or use of one or more products. It seeks to establish an international framework for data exchange and information sharing by providing a common messaging format for transmitting ICSRs relating to these issues.
The messaging format is based on the HL7 Reference Information Model (RIM) and can be extended or constrained to accommodate a variety of reporting use cases. This standard will be harmonised with other HL7 public health and patient safety reporting standards to help ensure that messaging constructs and vocabulary are harmonised in the HL7 Public Health and Regulatory Reporting domains.
The data elements used in this standard were identified as consistent across many of the use cases and can be applied to a variety of reporting scenarios. Specific reporting requirements within organisations or regions might vary.
This standard is part 1 of 2 in Health informatics — Individual case safety reports (ICSRs) in pharmacovigilance.

title: Health informatics — Guidelines on data protection to facilitate transborder flows of personal health data
description: This standard provides guidance on data protection requirements to facilitate the transfer of personal health data across national or jurisdictional borders.
body:
This standard provides guidance on data protection requirements to facilitate the transfer of personal health data across national or jurisdictional borders. It is normative only in respect of international or trans-jurisdictional exchange of personal health data. However, it can be informative with respect to the protection of health information within national/jurisdictional boundaries and provide assistance to national or jurisdictional bodies involved in the development and implementation of data protection principles. The standard covers both the data protection principles that apply to international or trans-jurisdictional transfers and the security policy which an organisation adopts to ensure compliance with those principles. The standard aims to facilitate international and trans-jurisdictional health-related applications involving the transfer of personal health data. It seeks to provide the means by which health data relating to data subjects, such as patients, will be adequately protected when sent to, and processed in, another country/jurisdiction.

title: Business requirements for health summary records – Part 1: Requirements
description: This document defines and describes health summary records based on a comprehensive review a series of initiatives and implementations worldwide.
body:
A health summary record (HSR) is a health record extract comprising a standardised collection of clinical and contextual information (retrospective, concurrent, prospective) that provides a snapshot in time of a subject-of-care’s health information and healthcare.
This document defines and describes HSRs based on a comprehensive review of a series of HSR initiatives and implementations worldwide. It summarises the business requirements driving the development of HSRs, the content that is common across HSRs, and issues associated with HSRs. Future activities are also recommended for the ISO’s Technical Committee on health informatics (TC 215) to support international standardisation of HSRs.
This document focuses primarily on requirements that are unique to HSRs. It does not attempt to articulate, other than at a high level, requirements that are generally applicable to all health records or all electronic health records.

body:
Healthcare terminological systems (terminologies) are developed to support accurate representation, communication and analysis of information about the healthcare of individuals and populations.
This document specifies the principles and processes that should be followed by developers of healthcare terminologies to support international healthcare terminology standardisation. It complements standards such as ISO 17115 and ISO 17117 (which address the content of terminologies) by specifying good governance requirements for the lifecycle of those terminologies.
The target audience for this document includes organisations that develop terminologies, and those who review these organisations and evaluate the terminology services and products they maintain.

title: Health informatics — Principles of mapping between terminological systems
description: This document establishes and harmonises the basic principles for developing, maintaining, and using maps in healthcare. It provides guidelines for good practice to underpin the mapping process.
body:
Mapping in healthcare is a way to integrate different terminological resources used for different purposes. It can help bridge different terminologies when required for interoperability. For example, data collected for communicating information about direct patient care (using clinical terminologies) can be reused for statistical and administrative reporting of morbidity data (using clinical classifications) by transforming the terminological representations into classification representations.
This document establishes and harmonises the basic principles for developing, maintaining, and using maps in healthcare. It provides guidelines for good practice to underpin the mapping process and explains the risks involved in the mapping process. It also identifies variations in process, precision, and administration when mapping for different purposes and in different environments.
This document is for organisations charged with creating or applying maps to meet their business needs.

· Scope
· Terminological resources

title: Health Informatics — Dynamic on-demand virtual private network for health information infrastructure
description: This document describes the threats anticipated in a healthcare network, as well as how a dynamic on-demand VPN is applied in the healthcare field.
body:
This document describes the threats anticipated in a healthcare network, as well as how a dynamic on-demand VPN is applied in the healthcare field. It explains the network requirements in the healthcare field, the network security of an open network for the healthcare field, and the minimum guidelines for security management of health information exchange, including personal data, between external institutions.
These requirements will assist in understanding the operation of security and evaluation of security issues in the healthcare field, and the usefulness of a managed VPN, such as a dynamic on-demand VPN.
Examples are included of security measures taken in a dynamic on-demand VPN for exchange of medical information. These examples provide network solutions to potential risks in such a user environment. They do not specify the dynamic on-demand VPN itself.

title: Health informatics — Clinical stakeholder participation in the work of ISO TC 215
description: This report clarifies and confirms TC 215 support for clinical stakeholder participation. It also makes recommendations to the TC and national member organisations on approaches to improving clinical stakeholder participation, based on examples of existing effective participation models.
body:
This report clarifies and confirms the ISO’s Technical Committee on health informatics (TC 215) support for clinical stakeholder participation. It also makes recommendations to TC 215 and national member organisations on approaches to improving clinical stakeholder participation, based on examples of existing effective participation models.
The review is limited to clinical stakeholder groups and is based on informal consultation among delegates attending TC 215 meetings and email communication with interested individuals. There are 4 review areas covered in the report:
· stakeholder groups concerned with the work of TC 215
· potential benefits and outcomes of clinical stakeholder participation
· current nature of stakeholder participation
· recommendations for improving clinical stakeholder participation.
Opportunities to comment on the draft report were provided prior to, and during, the 2007 Montreal plenary meeting.

title: Health informatics — Use of mobile wireless communication and computing technology in healthcare facilities — Recommendations for electromagnetic compatibility (management of unintentional electromagnetic interference) with medical devices
description: This document provides guidance for the deployment, use and management of mobile wireless communication and computing equipment in healthcare facilities to mitigate electromagnetic interference (EMI) hazards.
body:
This document provides guidance for the deployment, use and management of mobile wireless communication and computing equipment in healthcare facilities to mitigate electromagnetic interference (EMI) hazards. This promotes effective electromagnetic compatibility among wireless technologies and active medical devices.
The recommendations in this document recognise the different resources, needs, concerns and environments of healthcare organisations around the world. Detailed management guidelines are given for healthcare organisations that desire full deployment of mobile wireless communication and computing technology throughout their facilities. In addition, suggestions are included for selective restrictions in cases where healthcare organisations have decided that comprehensive management procedures are not feasible, practical or desirable at the present time.

title: Health informatics — Common terminology services
description: This standard seeks to establish an international framework for the development of an application programming interface (API) that can be used by messaging software when accessing terminological content.
body:
This standard seeks to establish an international framework for the development of an application programming interface (API) that can be used by messaging software when accessing terminological content. It is not intended to be a complete terminology messaging service in and of itself.
This standard identifies the common functional characteristics that an external terminology must be able to provide. As an example, an HL7-compliant terminology service must be able to determine whether a given concept code is valid within the particular resource. Instead of describing a table keyed by the resource identifier and concept code, the CTS specification describes an application programming interface (API) call that takes a resource identifier and concept code as input and returns a true/false value. Each terminology developer is free to implement this API call in whatever way is most appropriate for them.

body:

It describes and defines the administrative data objects used within, or referenced by, patient-held health data cards using UML, plain text and Abstract Syntax Notation (ASN.1). description: This standard describes an IrDA-based, cable-connected local area network (LAN) for the interconnection of computers and medical devices.
body:

These communication services and protocols are optimised for use in patient-connected bedside medical devices. The standard describes an IrDA-based, cable-connected local area network (LAN) for the interconnection of computers and medical devices.
This standard is targeted to modifications of legacy devices but is also suitable for new device designs. The term legacy devices refers to equipment that is: already in use in clinical facilities; in active production at the facilities of medical device manufacturers; and beyond the initial stages of engineering development.

title: Health informatics — Health informatics profiling framework
description: This document provides a common description framework for health informatics standards artefacts – the health informatics profiling framework (HIPF).
body:
This document provides a common description framework for health informatics standards artefacts – the health informatics profiling framework (HIPF). The HIPF aims to provide a consistent method for describing and classifying artefacts within the domain of health informatics standards. It establishes common concepts and a vocabulary for describing the complex domain of various informatics standards initiatives and their supporting artefacts. This common means of description is necessary to facilitate the coordination, communication and comparability of health informatics standards across and between disciplines and jurisdictions.
Using the HIPF should promote the reuse of health informatics knowledge and improve the identification of opportunities for informatics standards alignment, collaboration and coordination.
The HIPF is intended to be used by developers and users of health informatics standards.

body:
This document specifies the way in which the Single European Code (SEC) should be formulated and printed on tissue and cell products labelled with International Society of Blood Transfusion Standard 128 (ISBT 128). This helps to ensure that the Donation Identification Number, Product Code, Division Code, and Expiration Dates are represented consistently in the SEC when it is applied to tissues and cells. The SEC is required on all tissues and cells regulated under Directive 2004/23/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council released for circulation in the European Union unless specifically excluded in the regulation. body:
Flag characters can be used for process control of 'critical points' during processing and distribution to enhance the safety of medical products of human origin. Critical points include labelling and relabelling of blood and blood components, and printing and attaching a record to follow specific units of blood or blood components.
This document provides an example of the use of flag characters in the Donation Identification Number (DIN) for process control of critical points during processing and distribution of blood products. organisation: International Council for Commonality in Blood Banking Automation (ICCBBA)
title: Implementation Guide - Use of the Processing Facility Information Code [Data Structure 033] v1.1.0
description: This document provides specific guidance for cellular therapy and tissue (including ocular) facilities to implement and use the Processing Facility Information Code [Data Structure 033].
body:
Multiple facilities may be involved in the collection or recovery, processing and labelling of a medical product of human origin. Donation Identification Numbers (DINs) contain the identity of the organisation that assigned the DIN (often the organisation that collects the tissue graft). However, there is also a need to record the facility involved in processing and labelling the final graft. The Processing Facility Information Code [Data Structure 033] is designed to electronically identify the facility responsible for labelling the finished graft as part of the product identification.
This document provides specific guidance for cellular therapy and tissue (including ocular) facilities to implement and use the Processing Facility Information Code [Data Structure 033]. The use of this data structure for tissues and cells is not currently required in most situations. However, future changes to how DINs are assigned and used may result in the need for this data structure for traceability purposes. In the meantime, some tissue and cell facilities may choose to implement this data structure for other reasons.
This document is a supplement to the ISBT 128 Standard Technical Specification (ST001).
Main sections:
- Background
- Processing Facility Information Code [Data Structure 033]
- When should this data structure be used?
- Traceability issues
- Standardised ISBT 128 Product Description Code versus facility-defined product code
- Product labels
- Information for software developers

title: ISBT 128 Standard - Technical specification v6.2.2
description: The ISBT 128 Standard is an international standard to convey information about medical products of human origin clearly and accurately. The standard is continually updated based on new and changing user needs.
body:
The ISBT 128 Standard is an international standard to convey information about medical products of human origin (MPHO) clearly and accurately. It was originally created as a labelling standard but has been redefined as an international standard for the transfer of information associated with MPHO. 
ISBT 128 provides for a globally unique donation numbering system, internationally standardised product definitions, and standard data structures for barcoding and electronic data interchange. The standard is continually updated based on new and changing user needs.

title: IHE Radiology Technical Framework Supplement – Cross-Enterprise Remote Read Workflow Definition (XRR-WD)
description: This document describes the Cross-Enterprise Remote Reading Workflow Definition (XRR-WD) Profile, which facilitates management of the Remote Reading Workflow.
body:
Remote Reading Workflow is the practice of having medical images from one organisation interpreted (read) by a reading specialist who is based at another organisation that may have different information sharing and image archiving systems. It is particularly important for smaller clinical institutions, including urgent care units, imaging centres, private practices and mobile imaging services with limited credentialed 24/7 staff to handle the reading workload.
This document describes the Cross-Enterprise Remote Reading Workflow Definition (XRR-WD) Profile, which builds upon the Cross-Enterprise Document Workflow (XDW) Profile to manage the workflow between facilities performing the workflow tasks. The document establishes a common set of rules for participants, presents typical use cases and demonstrates how to use a Workflow Document to track and manage this workflow.

title: IHE Radiology Technical Framework Supplement – Cross-Enterprise Document Reliable Interchange of Images (XDR-I)
description: This document permits a direct imaging document interchange between an imaging document source and other healthcare IT imaging systems.
body:
This supplement to the IHE Radiology Technical Framework v18.0 describes the Cross-Enterprise Document Reliable Interchange of Images (XDR-I) Profile, which provides DICOM Subject-Object Pair (SOP) instances and image reports using a reliable messaging system.
The XDR-I Profile permits a direct imaging document interchange between an imaging document source and other healthcare IT imaging document-capable systems. Content that can be shared using this profile consists of sets of DICOM instances (including images, evidence documents and presentation states) and diagnostic imaging reports provided in a ready-for-display format.
This supplement proposes changes to both Volume 1 and 3 of the Radiology Technical Framework.

title: IHE Radiology Technical Framework Supplement – Standardized Operational Log of Events (SOLE)
description: Healthcare providers’ efforts to increase throughput and efficiency depend on capturing workflow events and applying business intelligence tools. This supplementary resource defines a way to exchange information about business activities that can be collected, analysed and displayed using standard methods.
body:
Healthcare providers have a strong desire to increase throughput and efficiency, both to improve the quality and timeliness of care and to control costs. These process improvement efforts depend on being able to capture workflow events and apply business intelligence tools. This supplementary resource defines a way to exchange information about business activities that can be collected, analysed and displayed using standard methods. An example event sequence for a typical patient study might be ‘patient arrived’, ‘study started’, ‘study completed’, ‘patient departed’ and ‘report completed’.

body:
This supplementary resource specifies how DICOM stereotactic breast projection x-ray images are created, exchanged and used. It describes how acquisition modalities transfer the images and how image displays retrieve and make use of the images. It defines the basic display capabilities image displays are expected to provide, and which attributes should be used to implement those capabilities. The resource is designed to provide faithful and complete storage and retrieval of stereotactic breast projection x-ray imaging data, and sufficient display capability to allow optimal visualisation of images for the purpose of consultation or second opinion. This profile was retired in June 2024.

body:
This document describes the CT/MR Perfusion Imaging with Contrast (PERF) Profile, which facilitates the exchange of contrast-enhanced images containing standard attributes for timing and position.
This new profile allows for the interoperability of Contrast Perfusion imaging data encoded as Enhanced CT/MR DICOM objects. Previous applications depended on proprietary assumptions about the timing attributes expected to be present, and therefore were not generally interoperable.

description: This document describes the Imaging Object Change Management (IOCM) Extension, which assists healthcare and imaging centres in maintaining up-to-date and consistent versions of images.
body:
At healthcare and imaging centres, where images need to be shared among different systems, it is a common practice to distribute copies of imaging instances. It is also common to update, correct, split, combine, delete or otherwise modify studies or instances. This creates the potential for confusion, error or loss of data.
This document describes the Imaging Object Change Management (IOCM) Extension, which extends the IOCM Profile to support media interchange (PDI), study import 145 (IRWF) and cross-enterprise document sharing for imaging (XDS-I.b).

title: IHE Radiology Technical Framework Supplement – Import and Display of External Priors (IDEP)
description: This document describes the Import and Display of External Priors (IDEP) Profile, which allows for the automatic retrieval of images and reports throughout a shared network without the need for a common patient identifier.
body:
It is common for patients to move between healthcare facilities that serve a shared patient population. Many of these healthcare facilities already have a shared secure network infrastructure.
This document describes the Import and Display of External Priors (IDEP) Profile, which allows for the automatic retrieval of images and reports throughout the shared network without the need for a common Patient Identifier. The exchange includes ‘localising’ the data such that radiology images and reports may be displayed in the same system for side-by-side comparison.

description: This document describes the Encounter-Based Imaging Workflow (EBIW) Profile, which ensures that images acquired in the context of a patient encounter are accessible and fully integrated into the medical record.
body:
Medical imaging is increasingly done outside the context of an ordered procedure. This document describes the Encounter-Based Imaging Workflow (EBIW) Profile, which ensures that images acquired in the context of a patient encounter are combined with the corresponding metadata about the patient, the encounter and the performed imaging procedure.
The EBIW Profile facilitates the management of imaging data, including its link into the patient medical record and later access procedures. The profile specifies how to integrate the devices to capture appropriate context, populate relevant indexing fields, link to related data and ensure the images are accessible and fully integrated into the medical record.

title: IHE Radiology Technical Framework Supplement – Contrast Administration Management (CAM)
description:
This document describes the Contrast Administration Management 
(CAM) Profile, which automates the recording and storage of information about contrast administration during medical imaging processes.
body:
Contrast media is a valuable tool in diagnostic imaging. It is also used in image-guided biopsy and therapy to identify target structures, guide devices and assess therapeutic effect. However, administration of contrast can cause severe adverse reactions, including patient death, so it is important that medical record accurately captures all relevant details of the administration process.
This document describes the Contrast Administration Management 
(CAM) Profile, which automates the recording and storage of contrast information. The aim of this profile is to reduce the administrative burden on staff otherwise occupied with caring for patients. The CAM transactions are analogous to those in the IHE Radiation Exposure Monitoring Profile.
Main sections:

· Transactions: Store Contrast Information [RAD-138], Query Contrast Information [RAD-139] and Retrieve Contrast Information [RAD-140]

title: IHE Radiology Technical Framework Supplement – Radiology Remote Reading Workflow (RRR-WF)
description: This supplement introduces a Radiology Remote Reading Workflow (RRR-WF) profile which addresses use cases involving imaging studies that are distributed to other locations for interpretation and development of diagnostic reports.
body:
This supplement introduces a Radiology Remote Reading Workflow (RRR-WF) profile. The profile is intended to address a variety of use cases involving imaging studies that are distributed to other locations for interpretation of the study, and for development of diagnostic reports.
The profile specifies transactions for workflow management that are based on the RESTful worklist service defined by DICOM UPS-RS. The profile also specifies (as Named Options) transactions for accessing the inputs to the reading task, and storing the outputs from the reading task. The Named Options allow the use of XDS-I, RESTful DICOM, or Conventional DIMSE DICOM.

This supplementary resource is an extension to the Treatment Delivery Workflow - II (TDW-II) Profile. It deals with the recording of treatment sessions in case of system outages and/or crashes. It is applicable to managed treatment sessions only (that is, the treatment sessions have to be initially scheduled in the Treatment Management System and retrieved by the Treatment Delivery Device along the transactions specified in TDW-II). Standardising the recording process allows for safe continuation of the next treatment session after successful recovery of the system and the treatment artefacts.

description: This document describes the status of the opioid epidemic as of June 2021. Differences in public health policy are compared and contrasted to discuss the solutions used in different countries, jurisdictions, and territories.

description: The document describes an applied research effort exploring how various IHE profiles may be leveraged to support Integrated Care Pathways (ICPs).
body:
Integrated Care Pathways (ICPs) are long-running, orchestrated patient care plans that cross system boundaries. This white paper explores how Business Process Modelling Notation (BPMN) and IHE profiles may be leveraged to operationalise support for ICPs within the context of a Health Information Exchange (HIE).
The document describes an applied research effort exploring how various IHE profiles may be leveraged to support ICPs.
This document is intended primarily for those who make national-level funding decisions regarding healthcare. It will also be useful to developers, multilateral funders (or donors), health informatics thought leaders, researchers and members of standards organisations.

title: IHE Quality, Research and Public Health (QRPH) White Paper – Using IHE Profiles for Healthcare: Secondary Data Access
description: This document describes a standardised infrastructure that allows data sharing between clinical communities, which hold and use identifying patient information, and nonhealthcare organisations, which are not allowed to access identifying information.
body:
This document describes a standardised infrastructure that allows organisations that collect and use patient data for purposes other than patient care (such as research, education or public health) to access data and documents held by healthcare organisations and vice versa.
Such data sharing between a “Clinical Community”, the group of healthcare enterprises that work together to directly deliver patient care and require personally identifying patient information, and a ’Secondary Data Usage Community’ that is not allowed to access identifying information, requires a standard, reliable and secure de-identification process.
This document outlines how to build this standardised information-sharing infrastructure and discusses several use cases.

· Purpose, use cases and intended audience
· Concept of community
· Main architecture features
· Query definition and further architecture features

title: IHE Quality, Research and Public Health Technical Framework Supplement – Vital Records Death Reporting (VRDR)
description: This supplementary resource defines multiple approaches to capturing death reporting information from provider systems, including messaging, document exchange and a Retrieve Form for Data Capture-based approach. The resource also defines the transactions and documents that may be exchanged between jurisdictions and national statistics agencies.
body:
Vital statistics data aid decision-makers in setting policies, directing resources, managing problems and identifying emerging health trends. The provider caring for a patient at the time of death is responsible for reporting medical details on death. Some of the information to be reported exists in the electronic health record. This supplementary resource defines multiple approaches to capturing death reporting information from provider systems, including messaging, document exchange and a Retrieve Form for Data Capture-based approach. The resource also defines the transactions and documents that may be exchanged between jurisdictions and national statistics agencies.

description: This supplementary resource provides a profile for collaborative workflow or collaborative process management involving 3 main actors: the manager of process definitions, the manager of runtime processes and the performer of process activities.
body:
This supplementary resource provides a profile for collaborative workflow or collaborative process management involving 3 main actors: the manager of process definitions, the manager of runtime processes and the performer of process activities. It supports processes from domains involving collaborative workflows such as clinical research, quality, public health and patient care support. The profile defines the transport mechanism for process definitions but does not provide the definitions per se. Additional profiles that define specific processes will complement this profile and bring the process automation capability to completion. One of these profiles is the Clinical Research Process Content (CRPC) Profile.

title: IHE Quality, Research and Public Health Technical Framework Supplement – Quality Outcome Reporting for EMS (QORE)
description: This supplementary resource facilitates electronic data capture of quality measure data by emergency medical services to enable automated data capture and streamline quality measure analysis. This will improve the timeliness and accuracy of the information used to compute emergency medical services quality measures.
body:
New approaches to improving emergency medical services include leveraging clinical and process quality measures, including patient outcomes. Emergency medical services are unique as the only component of the healthcare system that brings care to the patient; all other components require the patient to come to the care provider. Emergency medical services often entail manual data collection, rather than using electronic information that is already collected in emergency medical services electronic patient care record systems and provider electronic health records systems. This supplementary resource facilitates electronic data capture of quality measure data to enable automated data capture and streamline quality measure analysis. This will improve the timeliness and accuracy of the information used to compute emergency medical services quality measures.

title: IHE Quality Research and Public Health Technical Framework Supplement – Prescription Repository Query (PRQ)
description: This supplementary resource integrates a prescription drug repository into an electronic medical records workflow to provide prescription drug information to physicians to they can make informed decisions on medication they should provide to their patients. This will help improve patient outcomes and prevent deadly drug-to-drug interactions.
body:
Since 1999 there has been a drastic increase in opioid use, which has become a problem in many countries. In response to this, many jurisdictions have been interested in creating prescription drug repositories to collect data on prescribing and dispensing of controlled substances. Providers, pharmacists and others can use the data in such repositories to address opioid overuse, drug diversion and physician shopping and prevent accidental, life-threatening, drug-to-drug interactions. This supplementary resource integrates a prescription drug repository into an electronic medical records workflow to provide prescription drug information to physicians to they can make informed decisions on medication they should provide to their patients. This will help improve patient outcomes and prevent deadly drug-to-drug interactions.

title: IHE Quality, Research and Public Health Technical Framework Supplement – Data Element Exchange (DEX)
description: This document describes the Data Element Exchange (DEX) Profile, which facilitates the repurposing of electronic health record (EHR) data for clinical research studies. The DEX Profile maintains a registry of correspondences between research and healthcare data elements, including terminology.
body:
A major barrier to repurposing electronic health record (EHR) data for clinical research studies is that information systems in the domains of patient care and clinical research use different schemas and terminology systems. This means the data within each system is stand-alone and not interoperable.
This document describes the Data Element Exchange (DEX) Profile, which adds mapping metadata to an annotated data capture form at the point of form design. This creates a metadata registry that defines and maintains correspondences between research and healthcare data elements.

description: This document describes the Aggregate Data Exchange-HIV (ADX-HIV) Content Profile, which supports interoperable reporting of HIV aggregate data.
body:
This document describes the Aggregate Data Exchange-HIV (ADX-HIV) Content Profile, which supports the interoperable reporting of HIV aggregate data. This reporting typically involves the initial submission of routine HIV reports from a health facility to an administrative jurisdiction and subsequent national and international-level reporting.
The ADX-HIV Profile provides a way to define a Data Structure Definition (DSD) and the normative schema of the aggregate data XML message to be exchanged between systems for a particular aggregate data report. This ensures consistency in aggregate data that are reported to different jurisdictions from the same source.

title: IHE Pharmacy Technical Framework Supplement – Uniform Barcode Processing (UBP)
description: This supplementary resource provides an interoperability mechanism for systems that do not implement barcode decoding algorithms to request decoding of barcode content. It describes the transactions between a system that has a barcode and needs it decoded and another system that decodes the barcode.
body:
The use of barcodes and other automatic identification and data capture in health care is increasing. Barcodes contain encoded data. To use these data, software systems need to decode the barcode, which is done by algorithms. This supplementary resource provides an interoperability mechanism for systems that do not implement barcode decoding algorithms to request decoding of barcode content. It describes the transactions between a system that has a barcode and needs it decoded and another system that decodes the barcode.
The resource is a supplement to the future IHE Pharmacy Technical Framework.

body:
IHE is an international initiative to improve the way computer systems in healthcare share information. 
IHE Integration Profiles offer a common language that healthcare professionals and vendors can use to discuss integration needs of healthcare enterprises and the integration capabilities of information systems.
This document describes the clinical use cases, actors, content module, and transaction requirements for Patient Care Device (PCD) profiles. title: IHE Patient Care Device Technical Framework Supplement – Point-of-Care Identity Management (PCIM)
description: This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Device Technical Framework describes the Point-of-Care Identity Management (PCIM) Profile, which allows for data exchange and synchronisation regarding the identity of medical devices collecting clinical information about a specific patient.
body:
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Device Technical Framework describes the Point-of-Care Identity Management (PCIM) Profile, which specifies HL7 v2 standard messaging for devices and IT systems at an acute care point-of-care.
The PCIM profile allows for data exchange and synchronisation regarding the identity of specific devices collecting clinical information about a specific patient. The profile has two main purposes: assisting in the association of collected data with the correct patient record and maintaining a ‘census’ of devices that frequently move between patients, such as infusion pumps and mechanical ventilators.

description: This document defines the Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) profile, which sets out a standardised means to transmit measurements from personal healthcare devices in a remote setting to a health care provider.

The Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM) profile sets out a standardised means to transmit measurements taken by personal healthcare devices in a remote setting to a health care provider, including remote home monitoring, sub-acute therapy devices and wearable technologies. Remote in this case means outside of a care provider facility and is typically in the patient’s home.

description: This document defines the Routine Interfacility Patient Transport (RIPT) profile, which enables the efficient and accurate communication of patient information when a patient is transported between facilities.
body:
When professional patient transport takes place between facilities, information necessary for the patient’s care needs to be recorded and communicated to the receiving facility. The Routine Interfacility Patient Transport (RIPT) profile allows for the efficient and accurate communication of such information via electronic transfer summaries.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework specifies the clinical document architecture (CDA) and Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) transactions that will be involved in interfacility patient transport.

· RIPT profile overview
· Actors, transactions and content modules
· Actor options
· Required actor groupings
· Use case #1: Hospital discharge to transport with information query
· Use case #2: Emergency transport from long-term care facility 
· Appendix A – Required data elements for transport summary

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: Retrieve Clinical Knowledge (RCK)
description: This document defines the Retrieve Clinical Knowledge (RCK) profile, which describes how health IT systems and information exchanges can retrieve clinical knowledge that is consistent and suitable for presentation to a clinician or patient.
body:
A wide range of web resources support access to clinical knowledge (e.g. about a specific disease, medical condition, set of symptoms or complaints, or medication) for both clinicians and patients. However, these resources have inconsistent structures and programming interfaces, making them difficult to integrate into healthcare IT solutions. 
The Retrieve Clinical Knowledge (RCK) profile describes how health IT systems and information exchanges can retrieve clinical knowledge that is consistent and suitable for presentation to a clinician or patient. The profile also provides a more complete implementation specification that will assist electronic and personal health records to integrate with new providers of clinical content. 
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework combines information presently found in standards and implementation guides from Health Level 7 International (HL7), the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) and IHE to describe a single interface that can be used by any system needing access to clinical content.
Main sections:
· RCK profile overview
· Actors, transactions and content modules
· Use case: patient education
· Infobutton knowledge request and response
· Security considerations
· Cross-profile considerations
· Appendix A – Infobutton implementation considerations

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: Query for Existing Data for Mobile (QEDm) v2.3
description: This document defines the Query for Existing Data for Mobile (QEDm) profile, which supports dynamic queries made on mobile devices (including tablets, smartphones, home health devices and large systems used in environments where interoperability requirements are simple).
body:
The Query for Existing Data for Mobile (QEDm) profile supports queries made on mobile devices regarding clinical data elements (including observations, diagnostic results, medications, immunisations and procedures) by making the information available to other systems within and across enterprises.
The term ‘mobile’ refers not only to applications on devices used for mobility that are resource- and platform-constrained (e.g. tablets, smartphones and embedded devices including home health devices), but also to larger systems deployed in environments where interoperability requirements are simple, such as pulling the latest summary for display.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework describes the QEDm profile, which is functionally equivalent to the QED profile but better suited for implementation via mobile applications. 
Main sections:
· QEDm profile overview
· Actors, transactions and content modules
· Clinical data consumer options
· Clinical data source options
· Use case #1: Discovery and retrieval of existing data elements
· Use case #2: Discovery and retrieval of existing data elements with source document links
· National extensions

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: Query for Existing Data (QED) v2.1
description: This document defines the Query for Existing Data (QED) profile, which supports dynamic queries for clinical data. This profile makes information widely available to other systems within and across enterprises to support the provision of better clinical care and reporting.
body:
A wide variety of systems need access to dynamic clinical information stored and maintained in an electronic medical records system or other clinical data repository. The Query for Existing Data (QED) profile makes the information widely available to other systems within and across enterprises to support provision of better clinical care, quality reporting, financial transactions, public health reporting, clinical trials, drug interaction checking and patient qualification for various protocols.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework describes how the QED makes the information widely available to other systems within and across enterprises to support the provision of better clinical care.

· Classification of information

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: Patient Plan of Care (PPOC)
description: This document defines the Patient Plan of Care (PPOC) profile, which allows Registered Nurses to exchange data related to creating and managing individualised inpatient patient care.
body:
The Patient Plan of Care (PPOC) is an individualised, mutually agreed upon plan that nurses use to coordinate inpatient care. The PPOC profile describes the contribution of Registered Nurses to the plan and provides a mechanism for the exchange of data related to creating and managing individualised patient care. The plan includes problem issues (nursing diagnoses), expected healthcare outcomes, implementable interventions, and evaluation of progress toward outcomes based on follow-up assessment.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework adds the PPOC Profile to Volume 1 of the Technical Framework and the Patient Plan of Care Document Content Module and related modules to Volume 2.

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: Paramedicine Care Summary (PCS) v1.1
description: This document defines the Paramedicine Care Summary (PCS) profile, which makes ambulance-based patient assessments and paramedicine interventions available electronically to hospital or emergency room staff, either when the patient arrives or in advance of the patient’s arrival to the hospital.
body:
When a patient is transported for a medical emergency to a hospital, inefficiencies and errors can occur if patient information is transmitted verbally or entered manually. The Paramedicine Care Summary (PCS) profile maps the flow of patient information from the ambulance patient record, commonly known as the electronic patient care record to the hospital electronic medical record.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework sets out how ambulance-based patient assessments and paramedicine interventions can be made available electronically to hospital or emergency room staff, either when the patient arrives or in advance of the patient’s arrival to the hospital.

· Actors, transactions and content modules
· Actor options
· Use case: emergency response for heart attack
· Security considerations

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: Multiple Content Views (MCV) v1.2
description: This document defines the Multiple Content Views (MCV) profile, which shows how text in clinical document architecture (CDA) documents can be tagged to allow different types of users to tailor the information shown to their needs.
body:
The viewing requirements of a patient differ from the requirements of a healthcare provider. The Multiple Content Views (MCV) profile provides guidance on how text in clinical document architecture (CDA) documents may be tagged to achieve different rendering behaviours. This allows one document to serve different needs based on the requirements of the person viewing the document.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework identifies many of the requirements expressed by patients, classifies these requirements, identifies the rendering behaviour associated with each, and provides guidance on how to tag CDA content in order to bind it to an identified behaviour.
Main sections:
· Multiple Content Views (MCV) profile

· Use case #2: referral to specialist

description: This document defines the labour and history physical (LDHP), labour and delivery summary (LDS) and maternal discharge summary (MDS) content profiles, all of which allow professionals to share patient information collected during labour, delivery and the immediate postpartum period.
body:
The information collected during labour, delivery and the immediate postpartum period is crucial for follow-up care. The labour and delivery profiles capture information such as delivery type, anaesthesia type, delivery and postpartum complications, maternal information and information about the newborn.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework outlines the labour and delivery history and physical (LDHP), labour and delivery summary (LDS) and maternal discharge summary (MDS) content profiles.

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: Guideline Appropriate Ordering (GAO)
description: This document defines the Guideline Appropriate Ordering (GAO) profile, which facilitates the communication of information regarding decision support guidance and allows receivers to evaluate the appropriateness of clinical decisions.
body:
The purpose of the Guideline Appropriate Ordering (GAO) profile is to support the communication of decision support guidance on whether an order is appropriate from electronic health record, hospital or departmental information systems. The profile allows receivers to evaluate the decision support that was used and determine the appropriateness of those orders.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework updates the PCC Technical Framework Volumes 1, 2 and 3 to incorporate changes necessary to add the GAO to the framework.

title: IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework Supplement: eNursing Summary
description: This document describes the eNursing Summary (ENS), which is an interoperable summary of nursing related data that communicates ongoing patient needs across different care providers.
body:
Transitions, or ‘hand-offs’, occur multiple times each day in a hospital and at every change of care location. The eNursing Summary (ENS) profile develops data sets related to a specific clinical environment as a process to create safe, effective communication. The process ensures continuity as the patient moves through care transitions.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework describes and provides use cases for the eNursing Summary content profile.

description: This document describes the Emergency Department Encounter Summary (EDES), which describes the content and format of records created during an emergency department visit.
body:
Emergency Department Encounter Summary (EDES) is a summary of the patient’s current health status and a summary of care rendered in the ED between arrival and ED departure. It typically contains content profile types including the triage note (TN), nursing note (NN), composite triage and nursing note (CTNN), emergency department physician note (EDPN), lab reports and diagnostic imaging reports.
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework sets out the EDES and associated content profiles.

description: This document defines the Dynamic Care Team Management (DCTM) profile, which allows patient care teams to dynamically share information and communicate about patient care.
body:
The Dynamic Care Team Management (DCTM) profile provides the means for sharing care team information about a patient’s care teams that meet the needs of providers, patients, payers and other stakeholders. In the case of patients with complex or chronic health conditions, it can be difficult to coordinate care among providers and caregivers. 
This supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination Technical Framework sets out the DCTM profile, which assists care teams in dynamically sharing patient care information and communicating about patient care.

description: This document outlines the CDA Document Summary Sections (CDA-DSS) profile, which is a content profile that describes the different types of section templates that can summarise content in the document (or add summary content that is not already included in the document).

description: The Deferred Response Option is needed when the Responding Gateway needs extensive time for processing (days or weeks). This option splits the request and response into 2 separate 2-way exchanges.
body:
This supplement specifies the Deferred Response Option for the Document Consumer, Initiating Gateway, and Responding Gateway actors in the Cross-Community Access (XCA) Profile. 
The Deferred Response Option is needed when the Responding Gateway needs extensive time for processing (days or weeks). The option splits the request and response into 2 separate 2-way exchanges. This requires applications to manage the message correlation, create application-level acknowledgements, and determine where to send the Deferred Response message.

title: IHE IT Infrastructure (ITI) White Paper – Analysis of Optimal De-Identification Algorithms for Family Planning Data Elements
description: This document describes the selection process for de-identification algorithms for the purpose of creating de-identified family planning data that can be used for health services research functions including program planning, budgeting and basic performance monitoring.
body:
This document describes the analysis performed by the IT Infrastructure Technical Committee when selecting the optimal de-identification algorithms to fulfil the De-Identification for Family Planning use case published in Volume 4 of the IHE Quality, Research, and Public Health (QRPH) Family Planning Trial Implementation Supplement.
This use case required the de-identification of data for the purposes of health services research, for example in program planning and budgeting, basic monitoring of program performance and adherence to the scope of a funded project, clinical quality improvement initiatives and whether services are being delivered to the intended populations.
The document has two intended audiences:
· clinicians, researchers, data analysts, and others who seek to understand how and why the selected de-identification algorithms were chosen for each data element (the target audience for this document)
· developers who will implement the de-identification algorithms into their software (who should start with the IHE QRPH De-Identification for Family Planning supplement and consult this document for additional background information where needed).

title: IHE IT Infrastructure Technical Framework Supplement: Cross-Community Fetch (XCF) Rev 1.6
description: The Cross-Community Fetch (XCF) Profile defines a single transaction for accessing medical data between gateways that facilitate multiple dimensions of communication.
body:
The Cross-Community Fetch (XCF) Profile defines a single transaction for accessing medical data between gateways that facilitate multiple dimensions of communication (trust, semantics, encoding, legislation, authority, etc.). The profile is integrates the Cross Gateway Query/Cross Gateway Retrieve transactions. 
In specific use cases - for example, when a few dynamically created documents need to be accessed from interacting communities with centralised data localisation - a single transaction (versus independent query and retrieve) may reduce the coordination and maintenance of the transactional dependencies and transaction states. 
For such use cases, and in environments where stateless Responding Gateways can be designed, it simplifies the implementation of Responding Gateways. However, it may increase the implementation complexity of Initiating Gateways serving some types of communities, such as XDS Affinity Domains. XCF offers a different deployment option from the general-purpose XCA Profile.

title: IHE IT Infrastructure Technical Framework Supplement: Care Services Discovery (CSD)
description: The CSD Profile supports queries across related directories that contain data about organisations, facilities, services and providers.
body:
The CSD Profile supports queries across related directories that contain data about organisations, facilities, services and providers. It can respond to queries such as:
- Which facilities are associated with which organisations?
- What services are provided at specific facilities or, conversely, where are the facilities that provide a specified service?
- Who are the providers associated with a particular organisation; what services do they provide; at which facilities do they provide these services, and when? 
- Within a specified date range, when are the schedulable time slots for the provider of a specific service?
Queries against an optional 'FreeBusy' service are also supported. This information supports the development of a list of schedulable time slots for providers or services at specific facilities.
The CSD Profile describes 4 actors and the transactions between them: Service Finder, Care Services InfoManager, Care Services Directory, and Service Availability.

description: This supplement introduces an Asynchronous Web Services (WS) Exchange stack based on the OASIS Applicability Statement 4 (AS4). The AS4 Specification provides a robust option to support cross-border e-health information exchange.
body:
This supplement introduces an Asynchronous Web Services (WS) Exchange stack based on the OASIS Applicability Statement 4 (AS4). The AS4 Specification provides a robust option to support cross-border e-health information exchange. 
Asynchronous processing supports scaling to large numbers of sources and recipients by allowing for more efficient handling of latency and scale. Examples of where this may be useful include the European eHealth Digital Service Infrastructure (which deploys the XCA, XCPD, and XCDR Profiles), and in countries that adopt OASIS AS4 reliable and secure messaging when cross-sector (beyond the health sector) applicability is needed.

title: IHE Eye Care Technical Framework Supplement – Key Measurements in DICOM Encapsulated PDF
subtitle: Trial Implementation
description: This document outlines the Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) 100 Encapsulated Document Information Object Definition (IOD), which allows eye care professionals to transmit the PDF reports generated by many eye care devices.
body:
Eye care devices commonly generate PDF reports that clinicians store in a patient’s medical record. Examples of these documents include visual field reports and Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) optic disc and retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL) reports.
The Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) 100 Encapsulated Document Information Object Definition (IOD) provides a specification enabling eye care implementers to transmit these displayable PDF reports, including numeric measurements that are critical for long-term patient care.
The initial scope of this option targets eye care clinical workflow automations to reduce or eliminate manual entry burden for long-term care of patients and for existing clinical quality measures programs. IHE Eye Care expects this list to expand based upon additional use cases.

subtitle: Trial Implementation
description: This trial implementation document describes the General Eye Evaluation (GEE) Profile, which defines the structure of data that is collected during a patient’s general eye examination.
body:
This document describes the General Eye Evaluation (GEE) Profile, which defines the structure of data that is collected during a patient’s general eye examination. This examination is an evaluation of the physiological function and the anatomical status of the eye, visual system and its related structures, plus additional patient information such as history, allergies, and social history.
This revised version of the original trial implementation is based on the HL7 Consolidated Clinical Document Architecture (C-CDA). It adds two content profiles: the GEE C-CDA Progress Note and the GEE C-CDA Consultation Note. The profiles are based on the Comprehensive 370 Adult Medical Eye Evaluation October 2010 Preferred Practice Pattern specification generated by the American Academy of Ophthalmology.

title: IHE Radiology Technical Framework Supplement 2006-2007 – Image Fusion (FUS) Integration Profile
description: Creating and viewing images that have been co-registered is becoming increasingly important. This supplementary resource addresses the ability to convey registered data from one system to another for further processing, storage and display and to present repeatable fused displays consisting of a grayscale underlying image and a pseudocolour overlay image.
body:
Creating and viewing images that have been co-registered is becoming an increasingly important process in the clinical domains and radiology in particular. Currently, registration and image fusion can be performed on individual workstations designed for that purpose, but the results are not readily transferable to other systems, often due to proprietary or inconsistent implementations. Further, for the results presented on different systems to appear the same, those systems must consistently and correctly handle issues such as identifying and retrieving the right datasets for the fusion display, matching data between single-slice and multislice datasets, performing spatial translations and rendering the fused display. This supplementary resource addresses the ability to convey registered data from one system to another for further processing, storage and display and to present repeatable fused displays consisting of a grayscale underlying image and a pseudocolour overlay image.

description: This document adds an option to the Scheduled Workflow (SWF) and Patient Information Reconciliation (PIR) Profiles to support the deployment of centralised long-term diagnostic imaging archives.
body:
Centralised (regional, national, or federated) multi-enterprise long-term archives for diagnostic imaging are increasingly being created and deployed. The use of these archives complicates how some existing actors and transactions are currently defined.
This document adds an option to the Scheduled Workflow (SWF) and Patient Information Reconciliation (PIR) Profiles. This new Multiple Image Manager/Archive (MIMA) option facilitates Image Manager/Archive transactions and allows an Image Manager or Archive to support multiple patient identifier assigning authorities.

· Volume 1 – Integration profiles: Scheduled Workflow (SWF) and Patient Information Reconciliation (PIR)
· Volume 2 – Transactions
· Volume 2x Appendix R.1: Multiple identity resolution use cases
· Volume 2x Appendix R.2: Multiple identity resolution transaction specifications
· Volume 3 – Cross-transaction specifications and content specifications

title: Quality, Research and Public Health (QRPH) Technical Framework Supplement – Physician Reporting to a Public Health Repository – Cancer Registry (PRPH-Ca)
description: This supplementary resource provides a means for electronic medical records systems in physician offices to report information on cancer patients to the public health cancer registry.
body:
Until recently, complete and high-quality cancer reporting has been achieved primarily through hospital cancer registries. Traditionally, cancer patients received diagnostic testing or work-up and/or treatment in hospitals. However, advances in medicine have allowed patients to now obtain care outside hospitals. Data collection systems from other sources (for example, physician offices) are not as consistent with reporting, leading to under-reporting of certain types of cancers. Reporting from these sources may also be done manually, which is very resource intensive, time consuming and vulnerable to transcription errors. This supplementary resource provides a means for electronic medical records systems in physician offices to report information on cancer patients to the public health cancer registry. A single, consistent method allows efficient and accurate exchange of information while reducing the burden on electronic medical record system-specific or registry-specific implementations.
Main sections:
· Volume 1 – Profiles: Physician Reporting to a Public Health Repository – Cancer Registry Profile
· Volume 2 – Transactions and content modules

description: This supplement to the Patient Care Device Technical Framework describes the Retrospective Data Query (RDQ) Profile, which allows clinicians to flexibly request information from retrospective stores of clinical data to support clinical decision-making.
body:
This supplement to the Patient Care Device Technical Framework describes the Retrospective Data Query (RDQ) Profile, which allows clinicians to flexibly request information from retrospective stores of clinical data to support clinical decision-making.
The RDQ can be used to compile reports on individuals or multiple patients at once and can include all retrospective data or that collected within a specific time interval. description: This document is a supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework V7.0. It contains 4 content profiles which are used to structure data about a pregnant patient who has been admitted for antepartum care immediately before or during labour.
body:
This is a supplement to the IHE Patient Care Coordination (PCC) Technical Framework V7.0. It contains content profiles which are used to structure data about a pregnant patient who has been admitted for antepartum care immediately before or during labour. This data includes information on the patient’s personal and medical history, antepartum ambulatory care, standard laboratory tests, and educational material provided during the visit.
This document covers the 4 profiles: Antepartum History and Physical (APHP), Antepartum Summary (APS), Antepartum Laboratory (APL), and Antepartum Education (APE). Antepartum Record (APR) is no longer a profile as it has been subsumed by Perinatal Workflow (PW) profile.
Each profile contains sections for purpose and scope, process flow, actors/transactions, options, groupings, security considerations, and content modules.

title: IHE IT Infrastructure White Paper: A Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) View of IHE Profiles
description: This white paper is for SOA practitioners who want to take advantage of the IHE profiles in their implementations. It shows how IHE profiles can be used in SOA design, and explores issues, challenges and benefits, with examples.
body:
This white paper is targeted specifically at SOA practitioners who want to take advantage of the IHE profiles in their implementations. It expands SOA in the healthcare domain with IHE's concrete approach to interoperability. It establishes terminology for comparing SOA and IHE approaches, and illustrates the use of IHE profiles in a hypothetical SOA design. The focus of the paper is to illustrate how IHE profiles can be leveraged in a SOA design; and to explore the issues, challenges and benefits of a closer alignment of the IHE Technical Framework with SOA approaches in the future. It uses realistic examples to provide practical use cases for IHE-based interoperability in an SOA.
Main sections:
- Introduction
- Comparing the approaches
- Service modelling by example
- Further issues for exploration
- Conclusion
