organisation: Independent Health and Aged Care Pricing Authority (IHACPA)
title: ICD-10 to ICD-10-AM
description:
Demonstrates the relationship between the codes in the World Health Organization’s ICD-10 and the Australian Modification of ICD-10 (ICD-10-AM).
The maps describe a directional relationship between a classification where the data is recorded in (the source classification) to the classification the data needs to be reported in (the target classification). The relationship between two classification systems is described using one of two possible types of maps: 
• One-to-one. This type of map is used where a code from the source classification is only linked with one code from the target classification.
• One-to-many. This type of map is used where a code from the source classification is linked with more than one code from the target classification, which may be of different types. Where a code has a one-to-many relationship, a new row is used to demonstrate this relationship. The source classification code is repeated against different target classification codes.
body:
Purpose:
Demonstrates the relationship between the codes in the World Health Organization’s ICD-10 and the Australian Modification of ICD-10 (ICD-10-AM).
The maps describe a directional relationship between a classification where the data is recorded in (the source classification) to the classification the data needs to be reported in (the target classification). The relationship between two classification systems is described using one of two possible types of maps: 

• One-to-one. This type of map is used where a code from the source classification is only linked with one code from the target classification.
• One-to-many. This type of map is used where a code from the source classification is linked with more than one code from the target classification, which may be of different types. Where a code has a one-to-many relationship, a new row is used to demonstrate this relationship. The source classification code is repeated against different target classification codes.

Use Case & Benefit:
Useful when Australian health data is recorded in ICD-10-AM but needs to be reported in ICD?10, compared internationally or when determining the comparability of the classifications. These mapping tables provide an insight into the enhancements made for ICD-10-AM.

organisation: Independent Health and Aged Care Pricing Authority (IHACPA)
title: ICD-9-CM/ICD-10-AM First Edition to ICD-10-AM Thirteenth Edition
description:
Provides the link between two editions of the classification.
Backward maps – provides equivalent code for new codes in the newer edition
Forward maps – provides equivalent codes for deleted codes in the newer edition
The maps provide an equivalent code that best matches the concept from a clinical perspective (called historical maps) as well as an AR-DRG perspective (called DRG maps – previously logical maps). Sometimes the historical map and the DRG map are the same code.
Multiple historical maps are provided where there are multiple codes that can be mapped from one edition to the next. The first historical map provided in the mapping table is the closest match to code descriptor, alphabetic index entries or relevant coding conventions.
 To map between multiple editions, users need to use all the mapping tables between these editions. For example, to map between Eleventh Edition and Thirteenth Edition, users need to use the mapping tables for both Twelfth Edition and Thirteenth Edition. Codes need to be mapped from Eleventh Edition to Twelfth Edition, then from Twelfth Edition to Thirteenth Edition.

Purpose:
Provides the map between MBS item numbers to the equivalent or best fit stem code in ACHI. ACHI is based on the MBS and further expanded to cover interventions not part of the MBS such as dental, allied health interventions, cosmetic surgery. For most codes the first five digits are the MBS item number, followed by a two-digit extension.

To assist in determining equivalence or best fit between ACHI codes and a subset of MBS item numbers.

title: International Classification for Nursing Practice (ICNP) - Translation Guide
description: The International Classification for Nursing Practice (ICNP) is an agreed terminology, or dictionary of terms or expressions, that may be used to represent nursing practice consistently in any setting.
body:
The International Classification for Nursing Practice (ICNP) is an agreed terminology, or dictionary of terms or expressions, that may be used to represent nursing practice consistently in any setting. It does this through statements that reflect what nurses observe and the care that they give. As an international terminology, it is essential that ICNP expressions are represented in a range of spoken languages to meet the needs of nurses globally and to ensure that the contribution of nursing to health and care is represented appropriately.
ICNP is an integral part of SNOMED. SNOMED has its own guidance regarding translation. This guide seeks to support the translation of ICNP independently from SNOMED.

This guide is an authoritative source to support the development and implementation of electronic record systems that use the International Classification for Nursing Practice (ICNP). ICNP is an integral part of SNOMED. This guide seeks to support the use of ICNP independently from SNOMED - that is, under a separate ICNP licence.

title: CDA R2.1 (HL7 Clinical Document Architecture, Release 2.1)
description: This standard is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of clinical documents for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients.
body:
A clinical document architecture (CDA) can contain any type of clinical content. A CDA document is a defined and complete information object that can include text, images, sounds, and other multimedia content. Typical CDA documents may include a discharge summary, imaging report, admission & physical, pathology report and more. The most popular use is for inter-enterprise information exchange, such as is envisioned for a US Health Information Exchange (HIE).
This standard is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of clinical documents for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients. It defines a clinical document as having the following 6 characteristics: persistence, stewardship, potential for authentication, context, wholeness, and human readability.

title: HL7 Domain Analysis Model: Common Clinical Registry Framework, Release 2
description: The Common Clinical Registry Framework (CCRF) Domain Analysis Model (DAM) describes the function, organisation, structure and major workflows of a general clinical registry.
body:
Registry data is typically highly specific and has a high level of structure. This means data collection often requires difficult and expensive custom interfacing. When source data are not readily available or transferable to registries in electronic formats, registries then must use manual data entry, which is inefficient and does not scale well.
This domain analysis model (DAM) describes the function, organisation, structure and major workflows of a general clinical registry. The DAM aims to express these aspects of clinical registries to those involved in the work of developing registries or making them interoperate with other health information systems.

title: HL7 Version 3 Product Suite
description: The HL7 V3 Normative Edition is a suite of specifications based on HL7’s Reference Information Model (RIM). It provides the full set of messages, data types and terminologies needed to build a complete implementation.
body:
The HL7 V3 Normative Edition is a suite of specifications based on HL7’s Reference Information Model (RIM). It provides a single source that allows implementers of V3 specifications to work with the full set of messages, data types and terminologies needed to build a complete implementation. 
The V3 specification is built around subject domains that provide storyboard descriptions, trigger events, interaction designs, domain object models derived from the RIM, hierarchical message descriptors (HMDs) and a prose description of each element. Implementation of these domains further depends on a non-normative V3 guide and normative specifications for data types, the XML technical specifications (ITS) or message wire format, message and control ‘wrappers’, and transport protocols.

title: HL7 Messaging Standard Version v2.7.1
subtitle: An Application Protocol for Electronic Data Exchange in Healthcare Environments
description: This document contains the specifications for Version 2.7.1 of the Health Level Seven Standard for electronic data exchange in healthcare environments, with special emphasis on hospitals.
body:
This document contains the specifications for Version 2.7.1 of the Health Level Seven Standard for electronic data exchange in healthcare environments, with special emphasis on hospitals. It serves as a way for different applications and data architectures within a similar system environment to communicate with each other. Specifically, it addresses the interfaces of various healthcare IT systems that send or receive patient admissions/registration, discharge or transfer data, queries, resource and patient scheduling, orders, results, clinical observations, billing, master file update information, medical records, scheduling, patient referral, patient care, clinical laboratory automation, application management and personnel management messages.
The standard includes updates made to support the US Government’s proposed Lab Results Implementation Guide functionality for reporting of lab results. Modifications or additions were made to Chapters 2B, 2C, 4 and 7.

title: HL7 Context Management Specification (CCOW), Version 1.6
description: This specification aims to facilitate integration of applications at the point of use. It serves as the basis for ensuring secure and consistent access to patient information from heterogeneous sources.
body:
This specification aims to facilitate integration of applications at the point of use. Context management ensures secure and consistent access to patient information from heterogeneous sources, by synchronising and coordinating applications to automatically follow the patient, user and other contexts. Context management may benefit healthcare facilities that have more than one system for clinical management.
This specification has been retired. It is being replaced by HL7 FHIRCast.

body: This document describes the Major Depressive Disorder Domain Analysis Model. The model enables standardisation of data elements for the clinical data necessary for evaluating marketing applications for drugs to treat major depressive disorder. It supports the collection of data during a patient encounter within a clinical research study. The data elements include concepts for DSM IV and 5 diagnoses and their specifiers as well as mental health specific patient characteristics, symptomatology, and history.

description: This document provides guidance for cooperative use of the Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) standard and the Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) health data paradigm.
body:
This document provides guidance for cooperative use of the Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) standard and the Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR) health data paradigm. The document describes the FAIR principles, which emphasise improving the ability of machines to automatically find and make use of research and other data, and support its reuse by human researchers. body:
This HL7 Version 3 standard: blood, tissue, organ; donation standard, Release 1 supports electronic interoperability activities related to the registration, screening for eligibility, donation and collection of blood from humans. The standard is a messaging specification intended to standardise the electronic transmission of information between system application components in a blood bank setting. The blood, tissue and organ domain comprises the models, messages and other artefacts that are needed to support messaging related to the process of blood, tissue and organ banking operations such as donations, eligibility, storage, dispense, administration/transfusion, explanation and implantation, but this release pertains only to blood donation.

body:
This standard supports the development of clinical decision support (CDS) knowledge artefacts. The approach is designed to be flexible and reusable, and to provide a baseline for CDS vendors and CDS knowledge artefact implementers. 
The specification defined in this document is based on a harmonisation of existing standards and specifications as the primary mechanism for structuring knowledge artefacts. It focuses not only on structure but also on semantics through the use of standard terminologies, value sets and taxonomies.
This standard serves as a companion document for the CDS Knowledge Artifact schema, and includes additional background, contextual information, and detailed documentation and guidance to support schema implementation.

body:
This document describes how HL7 V3 compliant messages can be expressed using XML. It presents an Implementable Technology Specification (ITS) for the encoding rules for HL7 Version 3 messages based on the Extensible Markup Language XML. This document covers ISO levels 5 and 6. Those familiar with HL7 V2 might call these the "XML encoding rules" for HL7 Version 3 messages.

title: HL7 Implementation Guide: Decision Support Service STU 1.2
description: This guide defines a decision support service (DSS) implementation approach that combines the HL7 DSS standard with other standards to facilitate implementation of a clinical decision support (CDS) system.
body:
This guide defines a decision support service (DSS) implementation approach that combines the HL7 DSS standard with other standards to facilitate implementation of a clinical decision support (CDS) system. Effective CDS systems bring together biomedical knowledge, person-specific data and a processing mechanism to generate and present information to practitioners, staff, patients and others at appropriate times.
The CDS standard uses the HL7 DDS standard, the HL7 Virtual Medical Record standard and the Simple Object Access Protocol (SOAP) and Representational State Transfer (REST) transport standards for web services.
The standards in this document were driven by United States requirements, but are expected to be useful and viable in other jurisdictions.

body:
An International Patient Summary (IPS) document is an extract from an electronic health record that contains essential healthcare information intended for use in the unscheduled, cross-border care scenario. It should include at least the required elements of the IPS dataset. The IPS dataset is a minimal and non-exhaustive patient summary dataset, specialty agnostic, condition-independent, but readily usable by clinicians for the cross-border unscheduled care of a patient.

title: HL7 Arden Syntax for Medical Logic Systems, Edition 3.0
description: This standard defines a syntax for creating and sharing computerised health knowledge bases.
body:
This standard defines a syntax for creating and sharing computerised health knowledge bases. The scope of this standard is limited to knowledge bases that can be represented as a set of discrete modules, each referred to as a Medical Logic Module (MLM). Each MLM contains sufficient knowledge to make a single decision. Clinicians, knowledge engineers, administrators and others can use the syntax to implement clinical decision support solutions to help improve quality and safety of care.
The main change in this version of the standard compared with the previous version is the addition of syntax to support Health Level 7’s Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) standards.

title: HL7 Version 3 Domain Analysis Model: Vital Records, Release 1
description: The Vital records domain analysis model (VR DAM) release 1.0 describes the workflows and stakeholders for transmitting birth and death data to and from US vital records systems. It also includes storyboards, a domain analysis model and supporting reference material.
body:
The Vital records domain analysis model (VR DAM) release 1.0 provides an initial overview of the US vital records domain of public health. It describes the workflows and stakeholders for transmitting birth and death data to and from US vital records systems. It also includes storyboards, a domain analysis model and supporting reference material. The VR DAM is based on the core set of vital records data elements that are on the US Standard Certificates of Live Birth and Death and the US Standard Report of Fetal Death. The supporting reference material includes the standard certificates, edit specifications and data-collection worksheets and guide to completing the worksheets. One of the work products for the VR DAM is a set of vital records storyboards developed to provide a better understanding of the vital records process.

description: This domain analysis model (DAM) incorporates the broad range of areas in public health related to immunisation. It provides a reference for mapping between HL7 immunisation artefacts and supports the needs of the clinical community regarding immunisation.
body:
Immunisation is an important component of preventative health care. Information about immunisation may be found on many different health information systems. Successful interoperable communication between these systems is required to optimise timely and appropriate immunisation. HL7 has developed a number of artefacts which support this interoperability. This domain analysis model (DAM) provides a reference for mapping between these artefacts and supports the needs of the clinical community regarding immunisation. This DAM incorporates the broad range of areas related to immunisation.

description: This resource is a bridge specification to allow a smooth transition between datatypes R1 and datatypes R2. It describes how HL7 V3 compliant messages can be expressed using XML and how the definition of the set of valid XML instance documents is derived from a specific HL7 Message Type.
body:
Data types are the basic building blocks used to construct messages, computerised patient record documents, business objects and their transactions. This resource is a bridge specification to allow a smooth transition between datatypes R1 and datatypes R2. It describes how HL7 V3 compliant messages can be expressed using XML and how the definition of the set of valid XML instance documents is derived from a specific HL7 Message Type. 
This resource intends to be mostly wire-backwards-compatible to existing ITS Structures R1.1 but conformant (directly or indirectly) to abstract datatypes R2 and ISO harmonised datatypes. It is very similar to ITS R1.1, but it adopts all changes and new features that have been introduced since R1.
It is a prerequisite that readers have a general knowledge of XML technology. The reader also should be familiar with HL7 version 3 concepts – especially those related to message definition.

title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Representation of the Health Quality Measure Format (eMeasure) Release 1
description: The Health Quality Measures Format provides a standard for representing a health quality measure as an electronic document. Quality measures provide an indication of an individual or organisation’s performance in relation to a specified process or outcome via the measurement of an action, process or outcome of clinical care.
body: A quality measure is a quantitative tool that provides an indication of an individual or organisation’s performance in relation to a specified process or outcome via the measurement of an action, process or outcome of clinical care. Quality measures are often derived from clinical guidelines and are designed to determine whether the appropriate care has been provided. The Health Quality Measures Format (HQMF) provides a standard for representing a health quality measure as an electronic document. Quality measures encoded in the HQMF format are referred to as electronic clinical quality measures (eCQMs).

description: This document describes a domain analysis model for pressure ulcer risk assessment and preventative strategies.
body:
Pressure ulcers are one of the most serious safety concerns related to hospitalisations, yet they are largely preventable.
Accurate identification of the risk of, and prevention strategies for pressure ulcers involves care coordination, patient health outcomes, care transitions, and effective preventative services.
This document describes a domain analysis model (DAM) for pressure ulcer risk assessment and preventative strategies. The DAM supports internal uses such as internal integration, quality control and decision support, and data exchange between providers and between providers and other stakeholders.
The model includes preliminary work on pressure ulcer interventions.

title: HL7 Version 3 Domain Analysis Model: Health Concern, Release 1
description: This document describes the Health Concern Domain Analysis Model which supports care coordination considering the perspectives of multi-disciplinary providers across care settings. It provides a way to relate events to a health concern and enable events attached to the health concern to be monitored.
body:
Healthcare delivery is complex. Patients, especially those with a history of multiple complex health issues, provide a care coordination challenge across institutions. Healthcare providers need a robust mechanism to clarify clinical findings, keep track of how comorbidities relate to and affect one another, and monitor the impact of different interventions on the progress of a patient’s conditions.
The Health Concern Domain Analysis Model supports care coordination considering the perspectives (concerns) of multidisciplinary providers across care settings. It provides a way to relate events to the health concern and enable events attached to the health concern to be monitored.

body:
A model definition language for clinical decision support (CDS) is a grammar for representing clinical information inputs and outputs that can be used by CDS engines and local clinical information systems. This model definition language is needed to enable the design and development of scalable CDS resources that can be used across multiple healthcare institutions and health information systems. GELLO is a standard language that can be used with the model to execute standardised clinical decision support logic.
This document defines a grammar for defining the classes and data types that can be used by a GELLO compiler or any other rule execution engine or authoring environment, to ensure good interoperability of CDS logic between institutions.

The exchange of information on oral diets, enteral nutrition (tube feeding and infant formula), oral nutritional supplements, food allergies, food intolerances and food preferences are required to provide inpatients and residents with nutritionally and culturally appropriate foods.
This domain analysis model covers the exchange of nutrition order information across the continuum of care. It describes the data and flow of information needed for the development of other HL7 products in the nutrition domain such as version 3 RIM-based nutrition order clinical messages, FHIR resources and profiles, and clinical document architecture nutrition templates.

title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Care Provision; Assessment Scales, Release 1
description: This standard describes the pattern an assessment scale should follow to enable uniform interpretation of scores of an assessment.
body:
Many score systems are used in health care on an international, national, hospital-wide or individual practice level. Other score systems are for scientific purposes. 
The representation of assessment scales, scores or indexes in information technology can be based on the Detailed Clinical Models approach, which requires a high level of standardisation when gathering data in the clinical context. Assessment scales require strict interpretation of what is being measured (the variable) and vocabulary used to describe the same clinical concept (the value set) to maintain validity and reliability.
This standard describes the pattern an assessment scale should follow to enable uniform interpretation of scores for an assessment. It enables any valid and reliable instrument such as scales, scores, indexes to be exactly represented in the HL7 v3 environment, allowing adequate communication of such data.

title: HL7 Domain Analysis Model: Health Quality Improvement, Release 1
description: This document is a conceptual data model that can be used as the basis for a logical data model for the domain of health quality improvement. It harmonises the elements from the existing electronic clinical quality measures (eCQMs) and clinical decision support (CDS) data models into a single, unified conceptual model.
body:
This document defines the Health Quality Improvement Domain Analysis Model (QIDAM) which is a conceptual model for health data for use in the health quality domain. It harmonises the requirements for patient data use in quality measurement and clinical decision support resulting in a single model to serve both aspects of quality improvement. By doing this, the model helps to overcome issues related to these 2 closely related artefacts being treated separately. 
Currently, the quality of care provided in measured using electronic clinical quality measures (eCQMs), and the interventions to improve the quality of care are measured using clinical decision support (CDS). These 2 artefacts are expressed using 2 different data models: the Quality Data Model (QDM) and the Virtual Medical Record (vMR), respectively. By harmonising the elements from the existing eCQM and CDS data models into a single, unified conceptual model, the QIDAM provides the foundation for consistency in format across eCQMs and CDS artefacts.

Main sections:

title: HL7 Domain Analysis Model: Harmonization of Health Quality Artifact Reasoning and Expression Logic, Release 1
description: This document defines the common concepts and semantics involved in modelling reasoning within the various aspects of the health quality domain, in particular, quality measurement and clinical decision support.
body:
This document defines the common concepts and semantics involved in modelling reasoning within the various aspects of the health quality domain. It describes the underlying concepts and behaviour necessary to enable the expression and accurate communication of health quality reasoning across the domains of quality measurement and clinical decision support. By doing this, it aims to provide a common conceptual foundation that other specifications can use whenever they need to express and communicate expression logic. In particular, this document contributes to the ongoing effort to harmonise expression logic representation between the 2 domains..

title: HL7 Version 3 Normative Edition, 2017, 10th Edition
description: The HL7 Version 3 (V3) Normative Edition is a suite of specifications based on HL7's Reference Information Model (RIM). It provides a single source that allows implementers of V3 specifications to work with the full set of messages, data types and terminologies needed to build a complete implementation.
body:
The HL7 Version 3 (V3) Normative Edition is a suite of specifications based on HL7's Reference Information Model (RIM). It provides a single source that allows implementers of V3 specifications to work with the full set of messages, data types and terminologies needed to build a complete implementation. The 2017 Normative Edition represents the complete suite of V3 specifications. Each of these specifications has been balloted to formal approval as either a Normative Standard, Informative Document or a Standard for Trial Use. It includes standards for communications that document and manage the care and treatment of patients in a wide variety of healthcare settings. Hence, it is a foundational part of the technologies needed to meet the global challenge of integrating healthcare information in areas such as patient care and public health.

title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Care Provision; Care Transfer Topic, Release 1
description: This resource covers the standard process to follow for all types of health referral requests, including acceptance and rejection messages.
body:
This resource covers the process for all types of health referral requests, including acceptance and rejection messages. 
It enables one health professional to ask another health professional to take over responsibility for the care of an individual. The professional receiving the request can accept or reject the transfer and give a reason for rejection. 
Using the specified interactions for this process guarantees a proper transfer of responsibilities. Data can also be added to referral or acceptance messages through clinical statements.

title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Care Provision Domain Information Model
description: The Care Provision Domain addresses the information that is needed for the ongoing care of individuals, populations, and other targets of care. This resource includes Release 1 of the Care Provision Domain Information Model, and the Care Provision; Care Record Topic.
body:
The act of ‘care provision’ is the referral of the patient to another provider for additional care. It clarifies exactly who is responsible for the care of a patient at a specific moment or care episode. The care record can be used to document the patient’s condition on transfer of care.
The Care Provision Domain addresses the information that is needed for the ongoing care of individuals, populations, and other targets of care. It describes information structures and vocabulary used to communicate information pertinent to the care of living subjects, devices, geographic sites, and other physical entities by a responsible care provider. 
This domain supports multiple referrals and record communication that support the collaboration and continuity of care between providers of care. 
This resource includes the HL7 Version 3 Standard: Care Provision Domain Information Model, Release 1 and the HL7 Version 3 Standard: Care Provision; Care Record Topic, Release 1, and other supporting processable files.

description: This standard defines messages exchanged with patient registries. The patient information model includes full information about the living subject playing the role of patient. body: This standard defines messages exchanged with patient registries. The patient information model is not limited to persons – any type of living subject can be registered as a patient. The model includes full information about the living subject playing the role of patient. It also includes relationships between the patient and healthcare providers who have primary care and/or preferred care responsibility for the patient.

title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Context Aware Knowledge Retrieval Application (“Infobutton”), Knowledge Request, Release 2
description: This specification provides a standard way for clinical information systems to request context-specific clinical knowledge from online resources. This type of knowledge retrieval tool is known as an ‘infobutton’.
body:
This specification provides a standard way for clinical information systems to request context-specific clinical knowledge from online resources. 
Known as an ‘infobutton’, this type of knowledge retrieval tool anticipates clinicians and patients’ questions based on the clinical context and provides links to resources that may answer these questions. This approach has been widely adopted to support clinicians to answer clinical questions that arise in the course of care.

title: Common Audit Message: An Informative Document for the HL7 Security and Accountability Special Interest Group
description: This informative document proposes privacy and security policy-relevant data that should be captured and communicated among application systems for auditing purposes.
body:
This informative document proposes privacy and security policy-relevant data to be exchanged among application systems via an audit message. It is intended to supplement existing system-specific security audits with healthcare application-specific requirements.
The document proposes several types of data that should be captured and communicated as evidence of compliance with, and violations of, a healthcare enterprise’s security and privacy policy. It also describes the data that would reside in a common audit engine or database and specifies how queries against audited events could be managed.

title: HL7 Version 2.9 Messaging Standard - An application protocol for electronic data exchange in healthcare environments v2.9
description: This messaging standard is widely implemented around the world for exchange of clinical data between systems.
body:
HL7's Version 2 messaging standard is the workhorse of electronic data exchange in the clinical domain, and is a widely implemented standard around the world. This messaging standard allows the exchange of clinical data between healthcare systems. It is designed to support data exchange in both central patient care systems and more distributed environments where data resides in departmental systems. Version 2.9 represents HL7's latest development in the line of Version 2 Standards that dates back to 1989.

title: HL7 Version 2.6 Implementation Guide: Newborn Screening for Critical Congenital Heart Defects (CCHD), Release 1
description: This implementation guide focuses on the transmission of critical congenital heart disease screening data from pulse oximetry devices to the public health program and other potential recipients (including providers and electronic health records).
body:
Many seemingly healthy babies with critical congenital heart disease (CCHD) may suffer negative health outcomes if the birth defect is not identified shortly after birth. Internationally, universal newborn screening for CCHD using pulse oximetry devices is promoted and/or mandated by program and legislative initiatives. When pulse oximetry devices can exchange data with information systems, the use of standards helps with the seamless exchange of data and improves patient safety. This implementation guide focuses on the transmission of CCHD screening data from pulse oximetry devices to public health programs and other potential recipients (including providers and electronic health records).

title: HL7 Version 2.6 Implementation Guide: Early Hearing Detection and Intervention (EHDI), Release 1
description: This implementation guide focuses on the transmission of hearing screening data elements from hearing screening devices to data recipients, which may be electronic health records or public health information systems.
body:
Early hearing detection and intervention programs help to identify children with hearing loss. Because newborn hearing loss is a neurodevelopmental emergency, children with hearing loss need to be identified shortly after birth to help them achieve better communication outcomes. This implementation guide focuses on the transmission of hearing screening data elements from hearing screening devices to data recipients, which may be electronic health records or public health information systems.

title: HL7 Version 2 Conformance Methodology Release 1
description: This standard explains the procedures and processes for constraining HL7 Version 2 message specifications, encompassing both message profiles and implementation guides that contain message profiles.
body:
This standard explains the procedures and processes for constraining HL7 Version 2 message specifications, encompassing both message profiles and implementation guides that contain message profiles. A message profile provides a precise and unambiguous specification of a single message definition. An implementation guide provides a broader context in which, typically, a set of message profiles is used to satisfy one or more use cases. The base HL7 Version 2 standard is a framework that contains numerous message events, and for each event it provides an initial message template (starting point) that is intended to be constrained for a specific use and context.

description: This ordering service interface provides a consistent, structured methodology for ordering a variety of services and products, including, but not limited to, pharmacy, nutrition, radiology and laboratory items.
body:
The ordering behaviour of clinicians can significantly affect the quality and cost of patient care. This ordering service interface provides a consistent, structured methodology for ordering a variety of services and products, including, but not limited to, pharmacy, nutrition, radiology and laboratory items. title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Care Provision; Food and Medication Preferences, Release 1
description: This messaging specification describes standards for the exchange of patient and resident preferences related to food and medication to support communication of these preferences between different systems and providers.
body:
This messaging specification describes standards for the exchange of patient and resident preferences related to food and medication to support communication of these preferences between different systems and providers. This Universal standard provides a better way to communicate patient food and medication preferences among applications and providers. It will help avoid situations where preferences are reported as allergies in order to avoid disliked foods or avoid medications that cause unpleasant side-effects.
Each entry is classified as food or medication and indicated as a positive or negative preference. Other information includes the stated reason for the preference such as religious, cultural, ethnic or moral beliefs.

title: HL7 Version 3 Standard: Orders; Diet and Nutrition, Release 1
description: This standard provides a messaging specification aimed at standardising the electronic transmission and exchange of nutrition orders in health care facilities across the continuum of care. It includes nutrition orders for oral diets, enteral nutrition (tube feeding and infant formula requirements) and nutritional supplements.
body:
This standard provides a messaging specification aimed at standardising the electronic transmission and exchange of nutrition orders in health care facilities across the continuum of care. It includes nutrition orders for oral diets, enteral nutrition (tube feeding and infant formula requirements) and nutritional supplements. The computerised food and nutrition management systems used by dietetics and food and nutrition services departments in hospitals and long-term care facilities depend on HL7 interfaces to exchange data with hospital information systems, electronic health record systems, and computerised physician order entry systems.
Parenteral nutrition is outside the scope of this standard, as orders for it require coordination with the pharmacy domain.

body:
OASIS EDXL-TEP is a messaging standard that facilitates the coordination of patient movement across the continuum of emergency medical care. The HL7 Messaging Standard is a messaging standard that hospitals use internally and in information exchange with other healthcare facilities. An EDXL-TEP/HL7 transform is needed to bridge the electronic gap between the emergency management, emergency medical services and hospital communities. This implementation guide guides the reader in the use of the accompanying spreadsheet that details the mapping and processing to transform an OASIS EDXL-TEP message into an HL7 ADT (and potentially RAS) message. It is aimed at software vendors engaged on either side of the interchange to provide alternative standard messages, both inbound and outbound.

title: HL7 Implementation Guide: Service-Oriented Architecture Implementations of the Context-aware Knowledge Retrieval (Infobutton) Domain, Release 1
description: Retrieving context-aware knowledge into clinical information systems is an approach for delivering relevant clinical knowledge to clinicians and educational material for patients at the point of care (Infobuttons). This implementation guide provides a specification for the Context-Aware Knowledge Retrieval (Infobutton) Standard.
body:
Online resources for clinicians and patients are now widely available, but there are barriers that prevent effective and frequent use of these resources to support decision-making. Retrieving context-aware knowledge into clinical information systems is an approach for delivering relevant clinical knowledge to clinicians and educational material for patients at the point of care. These kinds of tools are known as Infobuttons. 
This implementation guide provides a service-oriented architecture (SOA) specification for the Context-Aware Knowledge Retrieval (Infobutton) Standard. The specification is based on the RESTful (Representational State Transfer) software architecture style and uses the IETF ATOM content syndication standard as the basis for the knowledge response payload.

title: HL7 Informative Document: Patient Contributed Data, Edition 1
description: This white paper explores and defines Patient Contributed Data (PCD). PCD is data collected by patients and includes data from health devices, apps and sensors.
body:
This white paper explores and defines Patient Contributed Data (PCD). PCD is data collected by patients and includes data from health devices, apps and sensors. These data can be used to understand progress, deterioration, or cyclical variations in health conditions. It can also be correlated with other data to understand patterns.
This document aims to raise awareness and build strategies for additional standards and approaches to make PCD more available and accessible. It looks at barriers to effective use of PCD and recommends strategies for overcoming the barriers as well as ways to integrate this data for clinical use.
The audience for this document includes medical professionals, health technology professionals, standards developers, policy makers, as well as individual patients and their caregivers.

body:
This specification describes the requirements needed to manage terminology produced by a variety of organisations across the healthcare enterprise. It is intended to support standard terminology modules, value sets, and coding systems as well as local terms and equivalence mappings.
This document focuses on the use of a Terminology knowledge architecture (Tinkar) which creates a logical model to represent HL7 Codesets, SNOMED, LOINC, RxNorm, and other clinical terminologies. The Tinkar Reference Model is a logical model that describes the standardised model for terminology and change management. Tinkar provides an architecture that delivers integrated terminology to the enterprise and its information systems. In doing so, it addresses the differences in management and structure across reference terminology, local concepts, and code lists/value sets.

title: HL7 Informative Document: Gender Harmony – Modeling Sex and Gender Representation, Release 1
description: This specification proposes independent consideration of sex and gender in clinical care documents to improve health outcomes for gender-marginalised individuals and improve data accuracy for sex and gender information in health care systems.
body:
This specification proposes independent consideration of sex and gender in clinical care documents to improve health outcomes for gender-marginalised individuals and improve data accuracy for sex and gender information in health care systems. Assessing the differences between gender and sex promotes the health of women, men, and people of diverse gender identities of all ages. It also helps avoid systematic errors that generate results with a low validity (if any) in clinical studies. 
The Gender Harmony model describes an approach that can improve data accuracy for sex and gender information in health care systems.

title: HL7 Domain Analysis Model: THEMES (Terminology Harmonization in Exercise Medicine and Exercise Science) Framework
description: The THEMES (Terminology Harmonization in Exercise Medicine and Exercise Science) Domain Analysis Model (DAM) provides a harmonised conceptual view of the major concepts, data elements, and data interoperability requirements related to the use of physical activity and exercise outcomes in clinical applications and translational research.
body:
Physical fitness and exercise biomarkers are promising research and clinical tools for a wide variety of health conditions, ranging from osteoporosis in older people to asthma in children. Despite this promise, exercise-related outcome variables are used far more commonly in the research laboratory than in clinical trials or clinical practice due to terminological inconsistencies.
THEMES (Terminology Harmonization in Exercise Medicine and Exercise Science) is a domain analysis model (DAM) that provides a harmonised conceptual view of the major concepts, data elements, and data interoperability requirements related to the use of physical activity and exercise outcomes in clinical applications and translational research.

title: HL7 EHR Child Health Functional Profile (CHFP), Release 1
subtitle: Reaffirmation of ANSI/HL7 EHR CHFP, R1-2008 (R2014)
description: The Child Health Functional Profile (Child Health-FP) conforms to the HL7 electronic health record system functional model (EHR-S FM). It presents an HL7 Normative Functional Profile for electronic health record (EHR) systems that are used to care for children.
body:
The Child Health Functional Profile (Child Health-FP) conforms to the HL7 electronic health record system functional model (EHR-S FM). It presents an HL7 Normative Functional Profile for electronic health record (EHR) systems that are used to care for children. It provides the essential general paediatric functions and specific conformance criteria that should be included in any system through which a child might receive primary care in the United States in both inpatient and outpatient settings. The intent is to assist all childcare providers and associated IT vendors in helping to ensure the care of children through the safe and effective use of information technology. Specifically, the Child Health-FP describes additional EHR-S functionality that is necessary to care for a child aged 0–18 years who receives care for routine wellness and preventative purposes, acute illness, or acute trauma, when in the newborn nursery, the primary care provider's office, the emergency department or urgent care clinic, or the inpatient hospital setting.

title: HL7 EHR-System Public Health Functional Profile, Release 2
description: This HL7 Electronic Health Record System Public Health Functional Profile (PHFP) identifies functional requirements and conformance criteria for public health clinical information collection, management and exchanges that include specific public health programs (domains).
body:
This HL7 Electronic Health Record System Public Health Functional Profile (PHFP) identifies functional requirements and conformance criteria for public health clinical information collection, management and exchanges that include specific public health programs (domains). It covers the domains of Vital Records; Early Hearing Detection and Intervention; Chronic Diseases (Cancer Surveillance); Public Health Laboratory Interactions (Orders and Results); Occupational Disease, Injury, and Fatality; Health Statistics; Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism; Birth Defects; and Adverse Events.
The PHFP addresses the need to incorporate information exchange between direct care and public health settings when implementing healthcare information technology solutions. Meeting this need will reduce the burden for direct care providers to report to public health stakeholders and will improve the completeness and quality of data that public health agencies have available for delivery of direct care, care coordination between providers and agencies, and population-based disease surveillance.

title: HL7 CDA R2
description: The HL7 Version 3 Clinical Document Architecture (CDA®) is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of 'clinical documents' for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients.
body:
The HL7 Version 3 Clinical Document Architecture (CDA®) is a document markup standard that specifies the structure and semantics of 'clinical documents' for the purpose of exchange between healthcare providers and patients. It defines a clinical document as having the following six characteristics: 1) Persistence, 2) Stewardship, 3) Potential for authentication, 4) Context, 5) Wholeness and 6) Human readability. A CDA can contain any type of clinical content - typical CDA documents would be a discharge summary, imaging report, admission and physical record, and pathology report. The most popular use is for inter-enterprise information exchange. Release 2 is the previous version to Release 2.1.[LF21]

description: This document describes the Continuity of Care Document (CCD) specification developed collaboratively between HL7 and ASTM. It provides an alternative implementation to that specified in ASTM ADJE2369 for institutions implementing the HL7 Clinical Document Architecture (CDA).
body:
A continuity of care record (CCR) is a core data set of the most relevant administrative, demographic and clinical information about a patient's health care. It provides a means for one healthcare practitioner, system or setting to aggregate data about a patient and forward it to another practitioner, system or setting to support the continuity of care.
This document describes the Continuity of Care Document (CCD) specification developed collaboratively between HL7 and ASTM. It provides an alternative implementation to that specified in ASTM ADJE2369 for institutions implementing the HL7 Clinical Document Architecture (CDA). It describes the constraints on the HL7 CDA Release 2 specification in accordance with the requirements of ASTM E2369-05 Standard Specification for Continuity of Care Record (CCR).

title: HL7 CDA R2 Implementation Guide: Personal Healthcare Monitoring Reports, Release 1
description: The Personal Healthcare Monitoring Report (PHMR) is a document that carries information on personal healthcare monitoring, or data on measurements taken by patients on personal healthcare monitoring (PHM) consumer medical devices.
body:
The Personal Healthcare Monitoring Report (PHMR) is a document that carries information on personal healthcare monitoring, or data on measurements taken by patients on consumer medical devices. The PHMR is designed primarily for the automated reporting of measurements taken outside of healthcare provider facilities by personal healthcare monitoring (PHM) consumer devices that comply with the Personal Connected Healthcare Alliance (PCHA; formally Continua). 
PCHA-compliant devices are required to generate data with certain qualifications. Examples include weight scales, blood pressure cuffs, pulse oximeters, glucometers, thermometers, step counters, fitness equipment following the PCHA standard, and independent living sensor devices. The information is transmitted as notes and as raw data. Notes may be supplied by a disease management service provider. The information may have multiple characteristics, including representation of measurements captured by devices; representation of notes, summaries, and other kinds of narrative information that may be added by caregivers or by the users themselves; and representation of graphs that may be added by intermediary devices that represent trends of users' health.

This implementation guide supports the management of consent directives and policies, and provides a computable representation of a consent directive. It is a structured document specification to exchange signed consent directives and generate enforceable assertions or rules (e.g. SAML, XACML). This specification uses the concepts identified in the Composite Privacy Consent Directive Domain Analysis Model and the CDA® R2 specification.
CDA R2 supports the multiple representations of a consent directive as a narrative signed document (wet signature), and computable statements/entries using standard-based terminology. This implementation guide expands IHE BPPC (Basic Patient Privacy Consent) to include support for granular consent preferences and embedded alternative representations of consent.

description: This guide provides a plain-language healthcare vocabulary for patients. It is intended for user interfaces for healthcare consumers that create outputs for consumers to use, such as consent directives, reports of disclosures and notices of privacy practices.
body:
This guide provides a plain-language healthcare vocabulary for patients. This vocabulary is specifically intended for user interfaces for healthcare consumers that create outputs for consumers to use, such as consent directives, reports of disclosures, and notices of privacy practices. 
The technical or legal jargon used in documents available from these interfaces makes it difficult for patients to fully participate in decisions about their healthcare. Using plain-language vocabulary mapped to technical and legal terms increases the likelihood that patients will understand the choices they make, while ensuring that their choices are correctly translated across the system.

This implementation guide provides a summary of Arden syntax, as well as ideas and examples related to how the syntax may be best used to address certain clinical applications. title: HL7 Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF): Canonical Definition Specification, Release 2
description: This document provides the ‘top-level’ specification of the Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF). SAIF provides consistency between all artefacts, enables a standardised approach to developing and implementing enterprise architecture, and a way to measure the consistency.
body:
This document provides the ‘top-level’ specification of the Service-Aware Interoperability Framework (SAIF).. SAIF provides consistency between all artefacts, enables a standardised approach to developing and implementing enterprise architecture, and a way to measure the consistency. SAIF is the framework that is required to rationalise interoperability of standards. It is an architecture for achieving interoperability, but it is not a whole-solution design for enterprise architecture management. The SAIF-CD is written for persons or organisations that are interested in implanting SAIF as an adjunct to existing (or planned) enterprise architecture frameworks. SAIF’s singular focus is the various dimensions and perspectives that are not associated with enterprise architecture per se, but rather with achieving predictable, scalable and effective interoperability between the various software components that collectively populate one or more enterprise architectures. Such implementations are most effectively done through the development of an organisation-specific SAIF implementation guide (SAIF IG).
Main sections:
· Governance framework
· Behavioural framework
· Information framework
· Enterprise consistency and conformity framework
· Interoperability specification matrix (ISM)
· Compliant SAIF implementation guides
· Appendix: ISM specification matrix, template and instance; Foundational principles; Defining a SAOF implementation guide; Governance implementation considerations; SAIF-CD adoption and adaption of existing and/or related work

title: HL7 Specification: Domain Analysis Model Specifications and Requirements – Canonical Definition, Release 1
description: This document provides the canonical definition of a domain analysis model (DAM) for use by architects, designers, and developers of HL7 conformant DAMs.
body:
This document provides the canonical definition of a domain analysis model (DAM) for use by architects, designers, and developers of HL7 conformant DAMs. A DAM is a collection of artefacts at the conceptual level that represents a well-defined subject-area-of-interest. This may include static/informational artefacts (for example, class diagrams and roles) and dynamic/behavioural artefacts (for example, activity diagrams, interaction/collaboration/sequence diagrams and state diagrams).

title: RCPA Pathology Terminology & Information Models — SPIA release (30 September 2024)
subtitle: Pathology Terminology and Information Models
description: This release updates the RCPA (Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia) pathology terminology and information models, now labelled SPIA (Standards for Pathology Informatics in Australia), under the National Clinical Terminology Service.
body: This package delivers terminology sets and information models used in Australian pathology, developed and maintained by RCPA under the SPIA framework. The 30 September 2024 release includes updates to the Requesting Pathology Terminology Reference Set and other core terminology sets mapped to SNOMED CT. These resources support standardised electronic communication of pathology requests, reports, and data exchange in clinical systems. The release is part of the National Pathology Terminology and Information Standardisation Plan, aiming to enhance interoperability and semantic clarity across health systems in Australia.

body:
Pathology specimen descriptions are required to enable the laboratory to conduct the right investigation and the pathologist to interpret and report on the results clearly and unambiguously.
This reference set contains terminology for recording the description of a specimen that is relevant to a pathology investigation and is required for the purpose of specimen collection, analysis or result reporting. It supports the accurate and unambiguous electronic communication and exchange of information relating to pathology result reporting between a pathology provider to a requesting clinician, or other approved recipient.
This reference set can be used within implementations of the Pathology Result Report SDT (Pathology Result Report Structured Document Template. Sydney: NEHTA; 2009. v1.0.)

title: Emergency department reference set
description: This reference set is a superset of the Emergency Department Reference Set (EDRS) suite. It provides terminology to support recording of presenting problems and diagnoses in emergency department settings in Australia.
body:
This reference set is a superset of the Emergency Department Reference Set (EDRS) suite. It provides terminology to support recording of presenting problems and diagnoses in emergency department settings in Australia. It contains all 4 reference sets that comprise the EDRS suite:
· Emergency department diagnosis in presenting problem reference set
· Emergency department diagnosis reference set
· Emergency department findings in presenting problem reference set
· Emergency department reason for presenting reference set.
According to feedback, the partitions between the reference sets in the EDRS suite are not well suited to clinical use. The superset may be more applicable for use at the clinical level while the partitioned suite remains linked to reporting.
This reference set supports the accurate and unambiguous electronic communication and exchange of information between clinicians involved in a patient’s care relating to the patient’s presenting problem at the point of triage and diagnosis at the point of discharge from an emergency department.

title: Western Australia Health Schedule 4 restricted medicines for public hospitals reference set
description: This reference set supports the identification of the Australian Medicines Terminology Trade Product Unit of Use, Containered Trade Product Pack, Medicinal Product Pack and Medicinal Product Unit of Use concepts that have additional storage and handling requirements in public hospitals within Western Australia Health (WA Health), as per WA Health policies.
body:
This reference set supports the identification of the Australian Medicines Terminology (AMT) Trade Product Unit of Use (TPUU), Containered Trade Product Pack (CTPP), Medicinal Product Pack (MPP) and Medicinal Product Unit of Use (MPUU) concepts that have additional storage and handling requirements in public hospitals within Western Australia Health(WA Health), as per WA Health policies.
This reference set can be used by applications to identify Schedule 4 medicines which have additional storage and handling requirements within WA public hospitals, as per WA Health policies. The reference set includes medicines registered on the Australian Register of Therapeutic Goods, which contain any of the active ingredients: codeine, propofol, tramadol, zolpidem, zopiclone, and certain benzodiazepines (bromazepam, clobazam, clonazepam, diazepam, lorazepam, midazolam, nitrazepam, oxazepam and temazepam).
This reference set is not the same as a Schedule 4 Reportable medicines (as defined in Section 77 of the Medicines and Poisons Act 2014) reference set and does not have any use related to real-time prescription monitoring (RTPM) programs.

title: List of Medicines for Brand Consideration reference set
description: This reference set supports the identification of Australian Medicines Terminology Medicinal Product Unit of Use and Medicinal Product Pack concepts associated with the List of Medicines for Brand Consideration that forms part of the Active Ingredient Prescribing initiative.
body:
This reference set supports the identification of Australian Medicines Terminology (AMT) Medicinal Product Unit of Use (MPUU) and Medicinal Product Pack (MPP) concepts associated with the List of Medicines for Brand Consideration (LMBC) that forms part of the Active Ingredient Prescribing initiative. The LMBC supports prescribers' decision-making regarding the inclusion of brand on a prescription.
Under the National Health (Pharmaceutical Benefits) Regulations 2017, the Repatriation Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme Instrument 2013 and MRCA Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme Instrument 2013, all Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme and Repatriation Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme prescriptions must be prepared by active ingredient, the brand may additionally be identified if a prescriber considers it necessary.
This reference set can be used by prescribing-software vendors to identify medications for brand consideration to meet Active Ingredient Prescribing requirements.

title: Global Model Number (GMN)
description: This supplementary resource describes the Global Model Number (GMN), which is the GS1 key used within databases (not on products) to identify a grouped family of products or a model.
body:
This supplementary resource describes the Global Model Number (GMN), which is the GS1 key used within databases (not on products) to identify a grouped family of products or a model. The GMN enables companies to uniquely identify the product model through the entire lifecycle of the product, from design to disposal. For example, a GMN can be used to identify products that are sold under different versions (for example, with different supporting languages), but are otherwise the same. Other examples include medical devices, for which the lifecycle can include extensive time and effort in conformity assessment before a product can be released on a market. In the European Union (EU), the GMN supports the implementation of the requirements of the Basic Unique Device Identification and Device Identifier (UDI-DI) under the EU UDI regulations on medical devices and in-vitro diagnostics devices. The supplementary resource also includes a link to the GMN generator tool, which can be used by GS1 member organisations to create valid GMNs.
Main sections:

title: Global Individual Asset Identifier (GIAI)
description: This standard defines the Global Individual Asset Identifier (GIAI), which is one of the 2 GS1 keys for asset identification.
body:
This standard defines the Global Individual Asset Identifier (GIAI), which is one of the 2 GS1 keys for asset identification. Companies can apply a GIAI on any asset to uniquely identify and manage that asset. This could be a computer, desk, vehicle, piece of transport equipment or spare part. Barcodes and EPC/RFID tags can be used to apply the GIAI on assets. Applied electronically, the GIAI enables a company to record individual assets in asset management and accounting systems across company boundaries. This document complies with the AU/ISO/IEC-1459-4, further information is available in the GS1 General Specifications Standard.

title: XML Clinical trials standard v3.6
description: This standard is the clinical trials component of the GS1 XML standards, designed to enable the exchange of information between different organisations, including the sponsor, distribution management entity, depot and clinical trial site.
body:
This standard is the clinical trials component of the GS1 XML standards, designed to enable the exchange of information between different organisations, including the sponsor, distribution management entity, depot and clinical trial site. In-scope business message standard (BMS) messages include despatch advices, inventory reports, kit status changes, receiving advices, requests for inventory reports, dispensing advices, inventory release files, shipment confirmations, shipment notifications and shipment requests. For each message type, the standard includes:
- a publication package of business message standard documents that define the business solution
- schemas that contain the XSD files defining the message structure and content
- an instance file that contains XML instance sample messages
- an HTML sample that contains HTML representation of the SML instance sample messages.
The standard is supported by an implementation guideline that details best practice implementation of GS1 standards for electronic messaging in the pharmaceutical clinical trial supply chain.
Main sections (BMS packages for):
- despatch advices
- inventory reports
- kit status changes
- receiving advices
- requests for inventory reports

description: This document provides a strategic overview of Electronic Product Code (EPC) RFID-based electronic article surveillance for disposable and/or reusable tags in retail environments.
body:
Radio-frequency identification (RFID) is used to identify and track tags that are attached to particular objects. RFID-based electronic article surveillance (EAS) is a technological method for deterring and detecting theft of consumer goods.
This document provides a strategic overview of Electronic Product Code (EPC) RFID-based EAS for disposable and/or reusable tags in retail environments. It shows where RFID-based EAS implementation is possible and approaches to enabling the use cases outlined using existing GS1 and EPCglobal standards.
This document is useful for project managers who want to implement an RFID-based EAS solution in retail operations, loss prevention department leaders, store operations, internal design teams, systems integrators and technical project leaders in retail operations, and suppliers providing merchandise to a retailer.

· Purpose of this document

title: The Application Level Events (ALE) Specification, Part I: Core Specification v1.1.1
description: This document is part 1 of the specification of the Application Level Events (ALE) interface, through which clients may interact with filtered, consolidated electronic product code (EPC) data and related data from a variety of sources.
body:
This document is part 1 of the specification of the Application Level Events (ALE) interface, through which clients may interact with filtered, consolidated electronic product code (EPC) data and related data from a variety of sources. The role of the ALE interface is to provide independence between the infrastructure components that acquire the raw EPC data, the architectural components that process the data, and the applications that use the processed data. This document specifies all interfaces that are part of the ALE specification using unified modelling language (UML) notation. Part 2 of the specification specifies XML-based wire protocol bindings of the interfaces.

title: AIDC Healthcare Implementation Guideline
subtitle: Implementing AIDC standards in healthcare to improve patient safety and supply chain efficiency
description: This implementation guide is intended to guide healthcare stakeholders through the process of implementing the GS1 System for Automatic Identification and Data Capture (AIDC) in healthcare.
body:
This implementation guide is intended to guide healthcare stakeholders through the process of implementing the GS1 System for Automatic Identification and Data Capture (AIDC) in healthcare.
AIDC Standards for Healthcare trade items are incorporated within GS1 General Specifications. The result is a voluntary standard providing Healthcare industry stakeholders with a common set of data and data carriers to be applied to medical products at every packaging level. This includes specific guidance on selection and use of appropriate GS1 Product Identification Keys, additional product and production data (for example, batch/lot, expiration date, and/or serial number), and GS1 Data Carriers (such as linear barcodes, 2-dimensional barcodes and radio-frequency identification (RFID) tags).
This document is intended for parties within the healthcare supply chain who are responsible for the selection of data and data carriers, the labelling of healthcare products, and parties that scan/read the data carriers.

title: Sup 244 - Frame Deflate Transfer Syntax
description: This supplement adds a new transfer syntax to the DICOM Standard, primarily for single bit segmentation encoding, which is otherwise not well supported.
body:
This supplement adds a new transfer syntax to the DICOM Standard, primarily for single bit segmentation encoding, which is otherwise not well supported. There is a need to be able to store and transfer encoded single frames (such as for DICOMweb) rather than the entire dataset for those applications where only selected frames of a multiframe object are required (such as for selected tiles at selected resolutions for whole slide images, or multi-organ segmentation of large volumetric CT or MR datasets). The scope is limited to Pixel Data as that is the primary use case.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Sup 243 - Label Map Segmentation
description: This supplement describes the addition of a Label Map Segmentation information object definition (IOD) to the DICOM Standard to encode classification of entities.
body:
This supplement describes the addition of a Label Map Segmentation information object definition (IOD) to the DICOM Standard to encode classification of entities.
The current DICOM Standard supports an IOD and service-object pair (SOP) class for pixel- or voxel-based segmentation encoding. This allows for overlapping of segments but it is inefficient and difficult to encode large numbers of non-overlapping segmentations. This is because they require non-trivial processing to extract from the bit-plane-encoded data to assure there is no overlap, and to convert to the commonly used label map form.
The new IOD reuses existing data elements for describing segmentations where appropriate. Segment properties are conveyed in the existing segment description structure in order to be compatible with the existing bit-plane-segment descriptions. The existing palette colour photometric interpretation may also be used (instead of monochrome).
The scope of this supplement is confined to label maps for classes (what class a segment represents) but not instances (which instance of a class is represented). Scope is also limited to a single label map, which does not allow for overlap of different segments. If overlapping of multiple label maps is required, separate SOP instances may be created.

title: Supplement 241: Structural Heart Procedural SR Template
description: This supplement introduces SR templates for Structural Heart Procedures to the DICOM Standard.
body:
This supplement introduces SR templates for Structural Heart Procedures to the DICOM Standard. These procedures involve interventions to address various conditions or abnormalities affecting the structures of the heart (excluding the coronary arteries). Unlike open-heart surgery, these interventions are minimally invasive or use a catheter-based approach.
As echocardiography (ECG) is the primary imaging modality used in periprocedural imaging, the templates proposed in this supplement are based the Simplified Adult Echocardiography Templates (root TID 5300). They are modified to support multimodality image acquisition.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 240: Height Map Segmentation and Revised Ophthalmic OCT En Face Image
description: This supplement introduces a new Height Map Segmentation information object definition (IOD) and service-object pair (SOP) class to the DICOM Standard.
body:
This supplement introduces a new Height Map Segmentation information object definition (IOD) and service-object pair (SOP) class to the DICOM Standard.
Height maps are 2-dimensional raster images which store surface elevations. This data can be applied to a 3-dimensional object. In DICOM, a height map is a type of segmentation using a 2-dimensional set of pixels to identify a surface in the 3-dimensional volume of a referenced multiframe image.
The Height Map Segmentation IOD follows the current enhanced multiframe image data architecture. For data management purposes, Height Map Segmentation SOP instances may be treated similarly to other segmentation images. The initial use case for the new IOD and SOP class is in ophthalmic tomography for representing segmentation of retinal layers. They are also broadly applicable for a variety of medical imaging domains.
This supplement also revises the current Ophthalmic Optical Coherence Tomography En Face Image IOD.

title: Supplement 239: Waveform Annotation SR
description: This supplement introduces SOP Classes to the DICOM Standard for storage and exchange of waveform annotations. It applies to all modalities in which waveform objects are created and applications are used to review them.
body:
This supplement introduces service-object pair (SOP) classes to the DICOM Standard for storage and exchange of waveform annotations. It applies to all modalities in which waveform objects are created and applications are used to review them. Waveform annotations can be stored in the waveform object itself expressing physical or environmental circumstances noted by the recording device at recording time.
This supplement adds a SOP class to store observations and measurements in a Waveform Annotation SR. It also defines a new Root Template derived from TID 1500, a waveform analogy to TID 1600 Image Library, and some included templates to store annotations as codes or free text and measurements. The Context Groups used in these templates are also defined.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 238: Assertion Collection
description: This supplement introduces the Assertion Collection information object definition (IOD) which provides a means to convey a collection of assertions for referenced DICOM instances.
body:
DICOM instances (such as images) are often collected and stored from different modalities for a single patient visit. There is a need to also collect the assertions related to these instances, such as approvals. This supplement introduces the Assertion Collection information object definition (IOD) which provides a means to convey a collection of assertions for referenced instances. It also allows for the collection of information about the clinical step in which the assertions were created, along with other meta information.
Potential use cases of the Assertion Collection IOD include collection of assertions for instance references during post-acquisition and pre-planning, treatment planning, treatment delivery, and pre- or post-treatment quality evaluation.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 237: General 32-bit ECG
description: This supplement defines a new ECG Waveform service-object pair (SOP) class to the DICOM Standard. It is based on the existing General ECG SOP class but with fewer constraints. The new General 32-bit ECG SOP class allows for 32 bits per sample.
body:
This supplement defines a new ECG Waveform service-object pair (SOP) class to the DICOM Standard. It is based on the existing General ECG SOP class but with fewer constraints. The new General 32-bit ECG SOP class allows for 32 bits per sample. This is an increase from the previous General ECG SOP class which can store waveform with 16 bits per sample.
Using 32-bit-data allows the new ECG waveform SOP class to be used with routine scalp EEGs and sleep studies, which commonly produce 32-bit data.

title: Supplement 236: Waveform Presentation State
description: This supplement adds the new Waveform Presentation State information entity (IE), and new service-object pair (SOP) classes to store Waveform Presentation States and the related Modules.
body:
This supplement introduces service classes for the storage and exchange of presentation information for DICOM waveform objects. It does this by adding a Waveform Presentation State information object definition (IOD).
The Waveform Presentation State object stores annotations, filters, and montages used for a given recording (patient related). A Waveform Presentation State object is stored together with the waveform study (such as a routine scalp electroencephalogram [EEG] recording) and can be exchanged between systems.
This supplement adds the new Waveform Presentation State information entity (IE), and new service-object pair (SOP) classes to store Waveform Presentation States and the related Modules.

title: Supplement 235: HTJ2K Transfer Syntax
description: This supplement adds HTJ2K (High Throughput JPEG 2000 image compression) Transfer Syntaxes to the DICOM Standard.
body: This supplement adds HTJ2K (High Throughput JPEG 2000 image compression) Transfer Syntaxes to the DICOM Standard. The addition of HTJ2K is intended to address some of the shortcomings of JPEG 2000 which have prevented wider adoption of JPEG 2000. The benefits of HTJ2K include making it easier to stream and decode thumbnails as well as improving the performance of encoding and decoding, and decreasing the encoder and decoder complexity.
