organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 234: DICOMweb Storage Commitment Service
description: This supplement defines the means to perform storage commitment in DICOMweb, via the DICOMweb Storage Commitment Service. The service allows a user agent to arrange the safekeeping of instances (such as DICOM images) on an origin server.
body:
This supplement defines the means to perform storage commitment in DICOMweb, via the DICOMweb Storage Commitment Service. The service allows a user agent to arrange the safekeeping of instances (such as DICOM images) on an origin server.
The DICOMweb Storage Commitment Service is an extension to the existing DICOMweb services. It mimics the storage commitment service that is already available using the DICOM Message Service Element (DIMSE), and has been designed to easily create proxies from/to DIMSE to/from DICOMweb Storage Commitment Service.

This supplement extends DICOM to add, and harmonise with, the HL7 Gender Harmony logical model and be consistent with the HL7 normative changes. This facilitates communication between DICOM and the various HL7 systems. This adds gender, sex, and related fields and resolves problems with the oversimplified single male/female (M/F) coding. This supplement includes updates the Patient Sex (0010,0040) description to match the HL7-updated definition. It also adds optional attributes to the Patient Study Module and to various C-FIND services. These optional attributes match those in the HL7 logical model.

title: Supplement 232: JPEG XL Transfer Syntaxes
description: This resource adds lossless JPEG recompression, lossless JPEG XL, and general JPEG XL Transfer Syntaxes to the DICOM Standard. JPEG XL is also added to the set of rendered formats for DICOMweb.
body:
This supplement adds lossless JPEG recompression, lossless JPEG XL, and general JPEG XL Transfer Syntaxes to the DICOM Standard. JPEG XL is also added to the set of rendered formats for DICOMweb.
The benefits of JPEG XL include improved compression of colour images and flexible encoding options. It also supports multiframe encoding more effectively than animated GIF (the only other multiframe rendered format).

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 229: Photoacoustic Imaging
description: Photoacoustic (PA) imaging is a non-invasive medical imaging modality. This resource introduces a new information object definition and a new storage service-object pair class for encoding and storing PA images.
body:
This supplement introduces a new information object definition (IOD) and a new storage service-object pair (SOP) class for encoding and storing photoacoustic (PA) images. PA imaging is a non-invasive medical imaging modality which creates an image based on the absorption of light in tissues. Contrast in the image is generated as different tissues and substances absorb light differently. PA imaging can identify intrinsic light absorbers such as haemoglobin and melanin, as well as extrinsic agents such as indocyanine green (ICG) or diverse types of nano-particles.
Prospective applications of PA imaging include classifying breast cancer lesions, screening sentinel lymph nodes, and assessing inflammation.

title: Supplement 228: Web Services for Volumetric Rendering
description: This resource introduces volumetric rendering web services to enable certain imagine techniques without having to specify numerous and complex parameters. It applies to volume rendering, maximum intensity projection, and multiplanar planar rendering.
body:
Imaging techniques to show 2-dimensional images of 3-dimensional datasets include volume rendering (VR), maximum intensity projection (MIP), and multiplanar planar rendering (MPR).
This supplement introduces volumetric rendering web services to enable VR, MIP and MPR without having to specify numerous and complex parameters. Web services enable a user agent to initiate server-side 3D volumetric rendering by specifying Query Parameters or a Volumetric Presentation State. This resource derives Query Parameters from Volumetric Presentation State attributes while maintaining alignment with current DICOMweb Studies Rendered Resources.

title: Supplement 227: Ultrasound Shear Wave Elastography Structured Report
description: This resource introduces a structured report section template for ultrasound shear wave elastography (SWE) results as well as a general ultrasound report in which it can be used.
body:
This resource introduces a structured report section template for ultrasound shear wave elastography (SWE) results and a general ultrasound report template in which the section template can be used. Ultrasound elastography is used on tissues including liver, breast, prostate and tendon. In SWE, the ultrasound system measures shear wave speed and derives a value for elasticity (in kPa) from that. Some systems also assess viscosity (which can be correlated to inflammation) by generating a value such as shear wave dispersion slope. Strain elastography is out of the scope of this resource.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 225: Multi-Fragment Video Transfer Syntax
description: This resource provides new transfer syntaxes that permit fragmenting video encoding. It responds to the appearance of videos larger than 2^32-2 bytes (for example, procedure video recordings), which previously needed to be split into multiple DICOM instances.
body:
This resource provides new transfer syntaxes that permit fragmenting video encoding. The transfer syntaxes correspond to 5 existing transfer syntaxes: MPEG2 Main Profile/Main Level Video Compression, MPEG2 Main Profile/High Level Video Compression, MPEG-4 AVC/H.264 High Profile/Level 4.1 Video Compression, MPEG-4 AVC/H.264 High Profile/Level 4.2 Video Compression and MPEG-4 AVC/H.264 Stereo High Profile/Level 4.2 Video Compression. The resource responds to the appearance of videos larger than 2^32-2 bytes (for example, procedure video recordings), which previously needed to be split into multiple DICOM instances.
HEVC/H.265 Main Profile/Level 5.1 and HEVC/H.265 Main 10 Profile/Level 5.1 already specify the use of more than one fragment so do not need to be modified.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 224: Service Discovery and Control
description: This draft resource provides mechanisms for discovering and managing processing services in the medical imaging space. It also covers support mechanisms for data exchange with a focus on transaction security.
body:
With the rise of artificial intelligence, containerised processing, service-oriented architecture and microservices, there are a growing number of processing services in the medical imaging space. Systems that use these services need to discover what services are available and to launch and control these services (for example, signalling a service to change its operational state). This draft resource provides mechanisms for discovering and managing processing services. It also covers support mechanisms for data exchange with a focus on transaction security.

description: This resource introduces a new information object definition (IOD) and a new storage service-object pair (SOP) class for encoding and storing dermoscopic images. Dermoscopy is a diagnostic technique that allows visualisation of the morphological structures of the skin.
body:
This resource introduces a new information object definition (IOD) and a new storage service-object pair (SOP) class for encoding and storing dermoscopic images. Dermoscopy (also known as dermatoscopy and epiluminescence microscopy) is a diagnostic technique that allows visualisation of the morphological structures of the skin. It is a non-invasive, in vivo skin examination. Dermoscopy is an important aid in the early recognition of malignant melanoma and other skin tumours but is also used for non-skin cancer disease conditions (for example, inflammatory disease).

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 214: Enhanced X-Ray Radiation Dose SR (including Cone-beam CT)
description: This resource creates a new information object definition (IOD) with the necessary flexibility to address cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) acquisitions. It defines a generic framework for describing radiation dose among the different CBCT uses.
body:
This resource creates a new information object definition (IOD) with the necessary flexibility to address cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) acquisitions. CBCT is used in several fields, and there are different methodologies for describing the dose associated with each use. However, the underlying data acquisition, reconstruction and testing parameters for image quality and dose evaluation are similar. This resource defines a generic framework for describing radiation dose among the different CBCT uses. There are 2 fundamental inclusions in the resource: decoupling of irradiation events and dose descriptions, and improved geometric description of the system. Radiotherapy treatment dose and radiopharmaceutical dose are out of scope.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: Supplement 212: XA Protocol Storage
description: This resource defines a pair of storage service-object pair (SOP) classes for distributing defined x-ray angiography (XA) protocols and recording performed XA protocols.
body:
This resource defines a pair of storage service-object pair (SOP) classes for distributing defined x-ray angiography (XA) protocols and recording performed XA protocols. The XA Defined Procedure Protocol Storage SOP Class describes desired values (and/or value ranges) for various parameters, which includes acquisition, reconstruction and storage tasks. The XA Performed Procedure Protocol Storage SOP Class describes the values actually used in a performed procedure. The SOP classes address details including patient preparation and positioning, equipment characteristics, acquisition technique, reconstruction technique, preliminary image handling and results data storage.
A similar pair of SOP classes were added for computed tomography (CT) in Supplement 121.

title: An Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
description: This document provides readers with an understanding of what artificial intelligence (AI) is and how it may impact the digital systems and services they use.
body:
This document provides readers with an understanding of what artificial intelligence (AI) is and how it may impact the digital systems and services they use. AI is the theory and development of computer systems able to perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making and translation between languages. Like all emerging technologies, AI presents both opportunities and risks. To take advantage of the benefits of AI securely, individuals and organisations should take some time to understand what risks apply to them and how those risks can be mitigated.

organisation: Australian Signals Directorate's Australian Cyber Security Centre (ASD's ACSC)
title: An Introduction to Securing Smart Places
description: Smart places are places that use smart technology (IT)-enabled systems to provide enhanced services to citizens. This document explores the key security risks of smart places and ways to mitigate risk.
body:
Smart places, also known as smart cities, are places designed to provide enhanced services to citizens using a collection of smart information technology (IT)-enabled systems and devices that capture, communicate and analyse data. Smart places vary in size and can refer to cities, suburbs, neighbourhoods, or an individual facility. A smart place often includes technologies such as Internet of Things (IoT) devices, operational technology, sensors and cloud computing services.
This document explores the key security risks of smart places and ways to mitigate risk.
Main sections:
- Security risks in smart places
- Internet of Things
- Supply chain
- Operational technology
- Cloud computing
- Mitigating security risk
- Operational redundancy
- Further information

title: Mergers, Acquisitions and Machinery of Government Changes
description: This document dicusses the signifiacnt challenges to cyber security faced by organisations when they undergo change.
body:
Major organisational change presents significant and unique challenges to cyber security, as they create upheaval and disruption to the normal flow of business. In short periods of time, new relationships need to be established, new business processes need to be integrated and systems need to be stood up, merged, relocated or decommissioned as capabilities are moved and consolidated.

The paper summarises some important threats related to AI systems and prompts organisations to consider steps they can take to engage with AI while managing risk. It provides mitigations to assist both organisations that use self-hosted and third-party hosted AI systems.
The guidance within this publication is focused on using AI systems securely rather than developing secure AI systems.

title: An Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
description: This document provides readers with an understanding of what artificial intelligence (AI) is and how it may impact the digital systems and services they use.
body:
This document provides readers with an understanding of what artificial intelligence (AI) is and how it may impact the digital systems and services they use. AI is the theory and development of computer systems able to perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making and translation between languages. Like all emerging technologies, AI presents both opportunities and risks. To take advantage of the benefits of AI securely, individuals and organisations should take some time to understand what risks apply to them and how those risks can be mitigated.
Main sections:

organisation: Australian Signals Directorate's Australian Cyber Security Centre (ASD's ACSC)
title: Cyber Security Principles
description: The cyber security principles provide strategic guidance on how an organisation can protect its systems, applications and data from cyber threats.
body:
The cyber security principles provide strategic guidance on how an organisation can protect its information technology and operational technology systems, applications and data from cyber threats. The cyber security principles are grouped into five functions:
- Govern - develop a strong cyber security culture.
- Identify - identify assets and associated security risks.
- Protect - implement controls to manage security risks.
- Detect - detect and analyse cyber security events to identify cyber security incidents.
- Respond - respond to and recover from cyber security incidents.
Organisations can also use a maturity model to assess implementation of the principles.

description: The Essential Eight is a set of cyber security risk mitigation strategies developed by the Australian Signals Directorate.

title: Maternity models of care v18.0
description: This report examines the different maternity models of care available across Australia in 2023, and the continuity of care within these models.
body:
About 1,000 maternity models of care are used across 251 maternity services. These fall into 11 model categories. This report examines the different models of care available across Australia in 2023, and the continuity of care within these models. It also uses Queensland perinatal data as a case study to explore, for the first time, the number of women using different models of care in Queensland, whether the models vary with maternal characteristics, and selected outcomes for mothers and babies by model of care. The report is a companion to Maternity models of care in Australia.

description: This dataset presents information on life expectancy and potentially avoidable deaths in 2015-2017 across Australia, by state and territory, Primary Health Network (PHN) and local areas (SA3).
body:
Life expectancy at birth is the number of years a person is expected to live at the time they are born. It is a broad measure of population health. Potentially avoidable deaths are deaths below the age of 75 from conditions that are potentially preventable through primary or hospital care. Rates of potentially avoidable deaths per head of population can be a useful indicator of how well health systems are performing. This dataset presents information on life expectancy and potentially avoidable deaths in 2015-2017 across Australia, by state/territory, Primary Health Network (PHN) and local areas (SA3).

organisation: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW)
title: Spatial distribution of the supply of the clinical health workforce 2014: Relationship to the distribution of the Indigenous population v1.0
description: This report used a new measure developed by the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare - the Geographically-adjusted Index of Relative Supply (GIRS) - to examine the geographic supply of the clinical health workforce in seven key professions, with particular relevance to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (First Nations) peoples.
body:
This report used a new measure developed by the Australian Institute of Health and Welfare - the Geographically-adjusted Index of Relative Supply (GIRS) - to examine the geographic supply of the clinical health workforce in seven key professions, with particular relevance to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (First Nations) peoples. The professions are general practitioners, nurses, midwives, pharmacists, dentists, psychologists and optometrists. The GIRS was developed to overcome limitations in using relatively simple provider-to-population ratios to compare areas with vastly different geographic characteristics. Areas with lower GIRS scores are more likely to face challenges in workforce supply than those with higher GIRS scores. The GIRS scores were compared with the distribution of the Indigenous population to assess the extent to which Indigenous people live in areas with lower relative levels of workforce supply.

organisation: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW)
title: Health checks and follow-ups for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people v1.0
description: Through Medicare, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (First Nations) people can receive annual health checks from their doctor - as well as follow_up services from other health professionals - that are specifically for First Nations people. These services aim to identify and treat common health conditions, such as diabetes and heart disease, that disproportionately affect First Nations people.
body:
Through Medicare, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (First Nations) people can receive annual health checks from their doctor - as well as follow_up services from other health professionals - that are specifically for First Nations people. These services aim to identify and treat common health conditions, such as diabetes and heart disease, that disproportionately affect First Nations people. The checks promote early detection and preventive care for these conditions. 
This report, updated biennially from 2024, presents the latest data and trends in the use of health checks and follow-_up services. The data are analysed by age, sex and geography. It also presents analyses of the use of Medicare Benefits Schedule (MBS) items for managing chronic disease in health-check patients.

title: Indigenous eye health measures 2021: in brief v1.0
description: This in-brief provides an overview of the latest data on eye health in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.
body:
Eye diseases and vision problems are the most common long-term health conditions reported by First Nations people. This in-brief provides an overview of the latest data on eye health in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. It summarises the findings from the annual report: Indigenous eye health measures 2021. organisation: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW)
title: National Housing and Homelessness Agreement performance indicators v10.0
description: These indicators are 2 of the performance indicators set out in the National Housing and Homelessness Agreement: Indicator (h), a decrease in the number of people that experience repeat homelessness; and Indicator (i), an increase in the proportion of people who are at risk of homelessness that receive assistance to avoid homelessness.
body:
People experiencing homelessness or situations placing them at risk of homelessness can receive assistance from specialist homelessness services (SHS). The National Housing and Homelessness Agreement (NHHA) aims to improve access to safe, affordable and sustainable housing, prevent and deal with homelessness, and support social and economic participation. 
The indicators presented here are 2 of the agreed national performance indicators set out in the agreement: Indicator (h), a decrease in the number of people that experience repeat homelessness; and Indicator (i), an increase in the proportion of people who are at risk of homelessness that receive assistance to avoid homelessness. These indicators are used to report progress against the objectives and outcomes of the NHHA. 
Note that the data cannot be interpreted as an estimate of prevalence of homelessness or population-level need for specialist homelessness services because interpretation of trends based on 4 observations may not be statistically robust. In addition, be cautious comparing indicators among states and territories - they may use different models of support delivered through the SHS system. Also, NHHA client cohort groups are not mutually exclusive and SHS clients may be included in more than one cohort group.
Main sections:
- NHHA performance indicators
- On any given day infographics
- Data

organisation: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW)
title: Child protection Australia 2021–22
description: This resource consists of an initial report followed by 3 updates, each focussing on a specific area of interest in child protection systems.
body:
The child protection system aims to protect children from maltreatment in family settings. This resource consists of an initial report followed by 3 updates, each focussing on a specific area of interest in child protection systems.
The initial report (Insights) provides and overview of children in child protection systems in 2021–22, including children subject to notifications, investigations, and substantiations of maltreatment, and the ways children were supported. The first update (Safety of children in care) presents data on the safety and abuse of children in care. The second update (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children) presents data on First Nations children in the child protection system. The data also include the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Child Placement Principle (ATSICPP) indicators. The third update (Pathways from out-of-home care) presents data on children leaving the out-of-home care system. The data also include the Permanency Outcomes Performance Framework (POPF) indicators and the National Standards for Out-of-Home Care (NOOHCS) indicators.

organisation: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW)
title: The Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Child Placement Principle indicators
description: The Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Child Placement Principle (ATSICPP) indicators present the latest data to assess progress towards implementing the ATSICPP. The ATSICPP aims to reduce the over-representation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in the child protection and out-of-home care systems.
body:
The Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Child Placement Principle (ATSICPP) indicators assess progress towards implementing the ATSICPP. Implementation of the ATSICPP by states and territories is designed to reduce the over-representation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in the child protection and out-of-home care systems. It also aims to keep Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children connected to their family, community, culture and country. At the core of the ATSICPP are the 5 elements of Prevention, Partnership, Placement, Participation and Connection. 
The ATSICPP indicators measure progress towards implementing the ATSICPP in child protection and out-of-home care systems and are grouped under each of the 5 core ATSICPP elements. The indicators presented in this report relate to the ATSICPP elements of Placement and Connection, and the reporting scope is limited to children living in out-of-home care.

organisation: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW)
title: Burden of avoidable deaths among Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people 2018 v1.0
description: This dataset presents estimates of avoidable deaths among First Nations people at the national and Indigenous Region level.
body:
Avoidable fatal burden is the burden due to deaths that are considered potentially avoidable given timely and effective health care. In 2018, almost two-thirds (64%) of the fatal burden among Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (First Nations) people was classified as avoidable. This dataset presents estimates of avoidable fatal burden among First Nations people at the national and Indigenous Region level.

title: The Unified Code for Units of Measure
description: The Unified Code for Units of Measure is a code system intended to include all units of measures being contemporarily used in international science, engineering, and business.
body:
In communication, it is important that all communicating parties use the same repertoire of symbols, and also attach the same meaning to the symbols they exchange. The common meaning must be computationally verifiable. The Unified Code for Units of Measure provides a single coding system for units that is complete, free of all ambiguities, and that assigns to each defined unit a concise semantics. 
The Unified Code for Units of Measure is a code system intended to include all units of measures being contemporarily used in international science, engineering, and business. The purpose is to facilitate unambiguous electronic communication of quantities together with their units. The focus is on electronic communication, as opposed to communication between humans. The Unified Code for Units of Measure are typically used in electronic data interchange (EDI) protocols, but they can be used in other types of machine communication.
Main sections:
· Grammar of units and unit terms
· Semantics
· Tables of terminal symbols
· Appendix A: Examples for some non-units
· Appendix B: Summary of conflicts
· Appendix C: Alphabetic index
· Appendix D: Example unit terms

title: Guide to HL7 V2 message parsing and management of character escaping with reference to backward compatibility
description: This technical report describes HL7 V2 message parsing and management of character-escaping with reference to backward compatibility.
body:
This technical report describes HL7 V2 message parsing and management of character-escaping with reference to backward compatibility.
HL7 V2 was created before XML was developed. As a result, HL7 V2 established its own encoding formats to allow complex hierarchical data structured to be encoded into text strings. It was also designed to allow backward and forward compatibility. Unlike XML, it does not add the field names to the message, and prior out-of-band metadata is required to read the values in the message. This makes it less readable by humans (compared with XML) but results in very small messages and is highly efficient. It requires careful parser implementation to use this format.
This report brings together the relevant information into one document to allow high-quality implementation of HL7 V2 parsers.
Main sections:
· Scope
· HL7 message encoding
· HL7 message structure
· Segments
· Datatypes
· Parsing HL7 V2
· Dealing with reserved characters and delimiters
· Free text formatting
· Unicode characters

title: Health informatics - Requirements for an electronic health record architecture v2022
description: This document describes the requirements for an electronic health record (EHR) architecture: the architecture of a system that processes, manages and communicates EHR information.
body:
This document describes the requirements for an electronic health record (EHR) architecture: the architecture of a system that processes, manages and communicates EHR information. The requirements aim to ensure that EHRs support healthcare delivery and good clinical practice, are clinically valid and reliable, meet legal and ethical requirements, and facilitate data analysis.
This document does not specify the full set of requirements that need to be met by an EHR system for direct patient care or for other use cases, but the requirements defined by this document do contribute to the governance of EHR information within such systems.
This document reproduces and is identical to ISO 18308:2011.
Main sections:
- EHR business objectives, for health systems, clinical practice and citizen inclusion
- Requirements for the representation of clinical information

This document describes how to analyse risks to quality, safety and continuity of care when telehealth services are used. The guidelines cover management of telehealth quality processes; strategic and operational process management relating to regulations, knowledge management (best practice) and guidelines; healthcare processes relating to people such as healthcare activities, planning, and responsibilities; management of financial resources; information management and security; and processes for planning and providing human resources, infrastructure, facilities and technology.
This document provides a set of example guidelines containing quality objectives and procedures for each domain. Organizations can apply the quality and risk management processes to develop their own telehealth quality objectives and procedures.

title: Health Care Client Identification
description: This standard is a voluntary code of practice. It provides a framework for improving the positive identification of clients in health care organisations.
body:
This standard is a voluntary code of practice. It provides a framework for improving the positive identification of clients in health care organisations. The objective of this standard is to provide the health industry with a specific standard for health care client identification for clinical and administrative data management purposes (data structure and specification). The standard promotes uniformly good practice in identifying individuals and recording identifying data so as to ensure that each individual’s health records will be associated with that individual only. The standard also provides the basis for future linkage of data as authorised by law and appropriate for clinical management of patients and statistical research purposes.
This document supersedes AS 5017-2002 and should be read in conjunction with HB 222 Australian Health Care Client and Provider Identification Handbook.

body:
Many countries systematically define and collect health information to monitor the performance of health systems. This often involves the collection of specific health indicators which are used to describe a variety of health and health-system-related trends and factors. A health indicator must be informative and also sensitive to variations over time and across jurisdictions.
This standard adopts ISO 21667:2010 to establish a conceptual framework for common health indicators. It is intended to foster a common vocabulary and conceptual definitions for the resulting framework.
Main sections:
· Scope
· Terms and definitions
· Health indicators conceptual framework
· Framework dimensions
· Annexes: A–H
· Bibliography
· Appendix ZA1: Introduction
· Appendix ZA2: Determinants of health
· Appendix ZA3: Health system performance
· Appendix ZA4: The impact of adoption of ISO 21667

description: This document describes the architecture of the openEHR Integration Information Model, which is designed for use in external systems and other situations where integration of data is needed.
body:
Getting data in and out of the electronic health record (EHR) is the main goal of openEHR. For new build situations, and for data being created via openEHR EHR platforms, there is no issue. In almost all other situations, existing data sources need to be accounted for. In general, external data have different syntactic and semantic formats than openEHR data, and conversion is needed.
This document describes the architecture of the openEHR Integration Information Model, which is designed for use in external systems and other situations where integration of data is needed.

title: Data Structures Information Model vRM 1.1.0
description: This document describes the common data structures used in openEHR reference models, including lists, tables, trees and history.
body:
Data in electronic health records (EHRs) must be expressed in standard structures. Such structures include single values (e.g. weight, height, blood sugar), lists of named or numbered elements (e.g. blood test results), tables of values with named columns and/or named rows (e.g. visual acuity results), trees of values (e.g. biochemistry, microbiology results) and histories of values, each of which takes any of the above forms (e.g. a time series of blood pressures, glucose levels, or imaging data).
This document describes the common data structures used in openEHR reference models, including lists, tables, trees and history. The intended audience includes standards bodies producing health informatics standards, academic groups using openEHR, the open source healthcare community, solution vendors, medical informaticians and clinicians interested in health information, and health data managers.
Main sections:
· Overview

· Representation package
· History package

description: This document contains the design specifications of the Guideline Definition Language version 2, which is a formal language for expressing decision support logic.
body:
Two main challenges to sharing clinical decision support content across sites are the lack of commonly shared clinical information models and flexible support for various language resources.
This document contains the design specifications of the Guideline Definition Language version 2 (GDL2), which is a formal language for expressing decision support logic in openEHR systems. It expresses clinical logic as production rules, which can be combined to support decision-making processes. These rules can be used to drive at-point-of-care decision support as well as retrospective populational analytics.
This specification helps overcome challenges in sharing clinical decision support content by providing a commonly shared clinical information model and flexible support for various language resources. GDL2 can support prompts, alerts and reminders; interactive single-screen decision support applications; detailed order-sets and care process support; algorithm-based calculators and more.
GDL2 introduces the following changes from the original GDL release:
· Data binding agnostic to EHR data models (e.g. openEHR / ISO 13606 / HL7 FHIR)
· Template definition for output objects with new use_template statement with template-level variables; this enables, for example CDS-Hooks Cards & Suggestions as result of rule executions
· Individual references and ability to reference to specific items in the rules
· More math functions support.
Main sections:
· Overview
· Requirements
· GDL2 semantics
· GDL2 object model
· Syntax specification
· Amendment record

title: Australian Core Data for Interoperability Release 1
description: This document outlines the contents of the Australian Core Data for Interoperability (AUCDI) and provides insights into the context of its development.
body:
This document outlines the contents of the Australian Core Data for Interoperability (AUCDI) and provides insights into the context of its development. The AUCDI is changing the approach to health data and is set to become a national asset focused on establishing an independent base of reusable, standardised information models and related artefacts. As clinical systems converge their internal data structures towards AUCDI, this common, consensus-based data foundation will reduce the need for data transformations and mappings, supporting safer and simpler interoperability.
The intended audience of this document is stakeholders interested in improving health data interoperability in Australia. This includes consumers, clinical and technical subject matter experts, healthcare organisations, peak bodies, technology and software industry partner organisations, jurisdictions, and government organisations.

title: GS1 Conformant Resolver — resolving GS1 identification keys to resources
subtitle: GS1 Conformant Resolver — resolving GS1 identification keys to resources
description: This standard defines GS1-Conformant Resolver (Release 1.1.0, Feb 2025), a protocol for translating GS1 Digital Link URIs into one or more linked resources (information or services) in a standardized and machine-discoverable way.
body:
The GS1 Conformant Resolver standard describes how resolvers should accept GS1 Digital Link URIs and return or redirect to relevant linked resources using HTTP. It supports decompression, validation, link metadata (like titles, relation types, media types), and fallback logic to ensure interoperability across distributed web systems.

By adhering to this standard, each GS1 identifier (e.g. GTIN, GLN) can serve as a bridge between physical entities and associated digital data or services — enabling scalable, linked supply chain, product, and health information systems.

title: EPCIS Standard v2.0
description: This document is the GS1 standard for EPC Information Services (EPCIS). It defines a data model for visibility event data and open, standard capture and query interfaces.
body:
This document is the GS1 standard for EPC Information Services (EPCIS). It defines a data model for visibility event data and open, standard capture and query interfaces. The goal of EPCIS is to enable disparate applications to create and share visibility event data, both within and across enterprises. This enables users to have a shared view of physical objects (for example, trade items, fixed assets, physical documents) and digital objects (for example, digital documents, electronic books), identified at a class or instance level, as each step of a business process is completed. EPCIS applies to all situations in which visibility event data is captured and shared. It is not restricted to electronic product code (EPC), radio frequency identification (RFID) or instance-level identification.

title: Healthcare GTIN Allocation Rules Standard v10
subtitle: GTIN Allocation Rules for the Healthcare sector
description: The Global Trade Item Number (GTIN) identifies regulated healthcare products (pharmaceuticals and medical devices) that may be priced, ordered or invoiced at any point in the supply chain, and about which there is a need to retrieve predefined information.
body:
The Global Trade Item Number (GTIN) identifies regulated healthcare products (pharmaceuticals and medical devices) that may be priced, ordered or invoiced at any point in the supply chain, and about which there is a need to retrieve predefined information. The Healthcare GTIN Allocation Rules help industry make consistent decisions about how to manage the unique identification of these product based on consideration of the product, its labelling and its packaging. 

The Healthcare GTIN Allocation Rules help ensure that the right product is supplied. They support operational efficiencies in the exchange of product information; the smooth operation of global supply chains; and global regulatory compliance. Overall costs are minimised while efficiencies and patient safety improve when all partners in the supply chain adhere to the rules. 

These rules are part of the GS1 system of standards and were developed in accordance with the GS1 Global Standards Management Process (GSMP). 

Main sections:
- GTIN allocation rules
- Clinical trials
- Additional GTIN information

title: GS1 Pharmaceutical Clinical Trial Electronic Messaging Standard Implementation Guideline
description: This guideline details the best-practice approach to implementing GS1 standards for electronic messaging in the pharmaceutical clinical trial supply chain.
body:
This guideline details the best-practice approach to implementing GS1 standards for electronic messaging in the pharmaceutical clinical trial supply chain. It helps resolve issues related to the lack of a standard for data exchange within the clinical trials environment and the potential data quality issues and supply chain disruptions that can occur as a result. This document helps to align the standards for data and information exchange with the direction of other industries, particularly the commercial healthcare supply chain.
This document is part of a suite of documents designed to guide readers on why and how to implement GS1 standards for electronic messaging. It defines the messages in scope, business-critical dataset and relevant business rules. Separate documents contain the detailed technical mappings to GS1 message formats.

body:
This guideline addresses issues concerning the presentation of products in online retail environments on small screens, typically alongside ‘add to basket’ functions. The work has been driven by the shift in online retail from desktop to mobile as consumers increasingly shop from their smartphones. The guideline is specifically focused on replenishment items, that is, products with which consumers are already familiar and therefore to which they will pay less attention when ordering online. Importantly, this rules out high-value or infrequently bought items over which a consumer is much more likely to spend significant time.

title: EPCIS and CBV Implementation Guideline v2.0
description: The implementation guide describes the use of EPC Information Services (EPCIS) and Core Business Vocabulary (CBV) to implement a visibility system to increase supply chain visibility. This document provides guidance at a foundational level.
body:
The implementation guide describes the use of EPC Information Services (EPCIS) and Core Business Vocabulary (CBV) to implement a visibility system to increase supply chain visibility. This document provides guidance at a foundational level. Specific industries, such as pharmaceuticals, have developed industry-specific implementation guidelines. EPCIS is a GS1 standard that defines a common data model for visibility data and interfaces for capturing and sharing visibility data within an enterprise and across an open supply chain. Its implementation is intended to provide users with a shared view of physical or digital objects within a relevant business context. Visibility data can describe the origin of an object, each location where it is subject to a business process throughout the supply chain or other process, when those processes took place and what was occurring to that object at each point.
Main sections:
- Overview of EPCIS
- Anatomy of an EPCIS event

- Data validation and system interoperability

description: The Secure Retrieve Profile defines a mechanism to convey authorisation decisions between actors in the community.
body:
The Secure Retrieve Profile defines a mechanism to convey authorisation decisions between actors in the community. This profile is limited to deployment models and policies where a central authorisation authority can make complete and definitive decisions, yet support federated identity and authentication. In these deployment models, no actors except the central authorisation authority need to know the fine-grain policies to enforce.
The supplement describes how to create a 'system of trust' between the actor that can perform authorisation decisions (on behalf of consent documents, policies and creation/access/disclosure rules) and actors that actually store clinical data and documents.

organisation: Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes (LOINC)
title: LOINC/RSNA Radiology Playbook User Guide
description: This document combines and unifies the useful aspects of LOINC Radiology and the RSNA (Radiological Society of North America) RadLex Playbook, both of which are designed to represent concepts of radiology orderables and results and their attributes.
body:
The LOINC/RSNA (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes/Radiological Society of North America) Playbook User Guide combines and unifies the useful aspects of LOINC Radiology and the RSNA RadLex Playbook. Both of those documents are designed to represent concepts of radiology orderables and results and their attributes.
Each term in the unified Playbook model takes on a number of attributes. This guide describes the semantics, syntax and proper use of those attributes. The attributes are used as building blocks to construct several types of standard names, including a fully specified name, long name and short name.
Main sections:
· Codes and workflows
· Syntax
· Modality
· Anatomic location
· View
· Timing
· Manoeuvre
· Pharmaceutical
· Reason for exam
· Guidance
· Subject

This document is the third subsection of section 12 of the Users' Guide for LOINC. LOINC is an international standard for identifying health measurements, observations and documents such as laboratory and clinical test results. 
Section 12 of the Users' Guide provides information to help users of LOINC make the best use of the standard. The LOINC code is a numeric code with a Mod 10 check digit. body:
RELMA (Regenstrief LOINC Mapping Assistant) is a free Windows-based mapping utility for searching the LOINC database and mapping local codes to LOINC terms.
The RELMA Auto Mapper Search increases the precision of a RELMA search by finding matching LOINC Parts (Component, System, Time Aspect, Property, Scale, Method) in the search string and then limiting the results to a specified type of LOINC. The use of this function typically results in the target LOINC appearing near the top of the search results.

organisation: Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes (LOINC)
title: LOINC Knowledge Base: Enriched Linkages between LOINC terms and LOINC Parts
description: This document describes the enhanced model of relationships between LOINC terms and LOINC Parts that was introduced in version 2.66. The model clarifies LOINC term labelling and defines 8 groups of LinkTypes.
body:
This document describes the enhanced model of relationships between LOINC terms and LOINC Parts that was introduced in version 2.66. These enhanced linkages are published as a LoincPartLink.csv file.
The new approach organises certain kinds of Part Types into 8 groups, each identified as a specific LinkType: Primary, DetailedModel, SyntaxEnhancement, SemanticEnhancement, Metadata, Radiology, DocumentOntology and Search. Not all LOINC terms will have all of these LinkTypes.
In the LOINC 2.68 release, the supplementary links were implemented as part of FHIR terminology services.

body:
This page contains links to 7 guides for using LOINC terms across 7 laboratory domains. These resources are designed to help laboratories map their individual tests or catalogues within these fields to appropriate LOINC terms according to current and best practices.
Each of these guides was written by LOINC's terminology development team and reviewed by external stakeholders and subject matter experts. After initial review, the guides underwent pilot testing by laboratory professionals, followed by a second external review. This process resulted in thoroughly vetted guides that can be used to train individuals in mapping lab tests to LOINC codes for interoperable data exchange.

organisation: Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes (LOINC)
title: LOINC Version 2.78
description: These zip files contain all of the core LOINC files, including the LOINC Table, documentation and all accessory files.
body:
LOINC (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes) is a freely available international standard for tests, measurements and observations. These ZIP files contain all of the core LOINC files, including the LOINC Table (all formats), documentation and all accessory files.
The core LOINC table provides the essential LOINC content in a format that is stable over the long run. The core table contains all of the LOINC terms that are in the complete table (the same number of rows) and a subset of the fields (different number of columns). The fields included in the core table are ones that are both crucial for defining each LOINC term and whose structure is not likely to change in the short term.
Main sections:

organisation: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (AIHW)
title: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Health Performance Framework
description: This dataset provides the latest information on health outcomes, health system performance and the broader determinants of health for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (First Nations) people, drawing from the Health Performance Framework (HPF) measures.
body:
This dataset provides the latest information on health outcomes, health system performance and the broader determinants of health for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander (First Nations) people, drawing from the Health Performance Framework (HPF) measures. Tier 1 measures health status and outcomes, Tier 2 measures determinants of health, and Tier 3 measures health system performance. This release incorporates updates from the following measures: Birthweight; Acute rheumatic fever and rheumatic heart disease; HIV, hepatitis and sexually transmissible infections; Infant and child mortality; Perinatal mortality; Contact with the criminal justice system; Child protection; Health behaviours during pregnancy; Antenatal care; Access to hospital procedures; Selected potentially preventable hospital admissions; Self-discharge from hospital.
Main sections:
- Demographic and social context
- How First Nations people are faring

- Tier 2 - Determinants of health

title: ICD-11 Reference Guide
subtitle: International Classification of Diseases for Mortality and Morbidity Statistics 11th revision
description: The ICD-11 is the latest (11th) revision of the International Classification of Diseases (ICD). This reference guide presents an introduction to ICD-11 and provides guidance on its use.
body:
The purpose of the International Classification of Diseases (ICD) is to allow the systematic recording, analysis, interpretation and comparison of mortality and morbidity data collected in different countries or regions and at different times. The ICD-11 is the latest (11th) revision of the ICD. This reference guide presents an introduction to ICD-11, including the context, components and intended use of the ICD-11. It describes the diverse components of ICD-11, provides guidance for certification, recording, rules for mortality coding (i.e. causes of death statistics) and morbidity coding (e.g. hospital statistics), and lists for tabulation of statistical data.

description:

body:
This instruction manual for the Tenth Revision of the International Statistical
Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems (ICD-10) contains
guidelines for recording and coding, together with much new material on
practical aspects of the classification’s use. This material is presented as a
separate volume so users can refer to the classification (Volume 1) and the instructions for its use at the same time. This manual provides a basic description of the ICD, practical instructions for mortality and morbidity coders, and guidelines for the presentation and interpretation of data. It is not intended to provide detailed training in the use of the ICD. The material needs to be
accompanied by formal courses of instruction on the use of the ICD that allow extensive practice on sample records and discussion of problems.

title: HL7 Cross-Paradigm Specification: FHIRPath, Release 1
description: FHIRPath is a path-based navigation and extraction language. Its operations are expressed in terms of the logical content of hierarchical data models, and support traversal, selection and filtering of data.
body:
In healthcare information systems, formal representation of logic is critical. FHIRPath is a path-based navigation and extraction language, somewhat like XPath. In FHIRPath, operations are expressed in terms of the logical content of hierarchical data models, and support traversal, selection and filtering of data. FHIRPath’s design was influenced by the need for path navigation, selection and formulation of invariants in both HL7 Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) and HL7 Clinical Quality Language (CQL).

description: This document helps define a minimal set of clinical research Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) profiles and elements for use in electronic health record (EHR) systems that can be used for real-world data in an interoperable and consistent manner for clinical research objectives.
body:
This document helps define a minimal set of clinical research Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) profiles and elements for use in electronic health record (EHR) systems that can be used for real-world data in an interoperable and consistent manner for clinical research objectives. In this context, real-world data refer to data captured in routine health care rather than data captured in controlled clinical trials. A number of case studies were used to determine what data elements would be needed. The standard is intended to provide data representations that will support real-world data research. It is also intended to facilitate downstream use for submissions to pharmaceutical regulatory agencies, but downstream use of retrieved data is not covered in this guide. title: HL7 FHIR Implementation Guide: International Patient Access v1.0.0
description: This document provides a specification for an application acting on behalf of a patient to access information about the patient from a clinical records system using a Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) Release 4 based application programming interface (API).
body:
This document provides a specification for an application acting on behalf of a patient to access information about the patient from a clinical records system (for example, a clinical care provider or national health records system) using a Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) Release 4 based application programming interface (API).
The International Patient Access (IPA) specification is designed to help patients access their data. The IPA profiles and the SMART App Launch specification can support clinician-facing applications and backend access to patient records. Applications that conform to IPA can access basic patient details, problems and conditions, medication orders and usage, immunisation history, allergies and intolerances, vital signs, lab results, and other clinical observations, as well as clinical notes and other patient documents.

description: This implementation guide defines mappings between FHIR® (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) release 4.0 and three specific CDISC standards: SDTM, CDASH and LAB.
body:
CDISC defines a number of standards that support the capture and sharing of information related to research and clinical trials. FHIR® (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is an HL7 standard for capturing and sharing healthcare information for a wide variety of purposes. 
This implementation guide defines mappings between FHIR release 4.0 and 3 specific CDISC standards: SDTM, CDASH and LAB. It is purely a 'descriptive' guide - it does not (currently) define any FHIR profiles, value sets or other artifacts. Instead, it provides mapping tables that show the mappings between elements in portions of selected CDISC specifications map to FHIR®.

The patterns are based on the Internet Engineering Task Force's (IETF's) OAuth 2.0 authorisation framework. SMART App Launch authorises a user-facing app to connect to a FHIR server and allows for launch context such as sharing the currently selected patient with the app, based on a user's session or selection at launch time. SMART Backend Services authorises a headless or automated client application to connect to a FHIR server without direct involvement of a user.

AU Core is provided to support the use of HL7 FHIR in an Australian context. It sets the minimum expectations on FHIR resources to support conformance and implementation in systems. title: The Application Level Events (ALE) Specification, Part II: XML and SOAP Bindings v1.1.1
description: This document is part 2 of the specification of the Application Level Events (ALE) interface, through which clients may interact with filtered, consolidated electronic product code (EPC) data and related data from a variety of sources.
body:
This document is part 2 of the specification of the Application Level Events (ALE) interface, through which clients may interact with filtered, consolidated electronic product code (EPC) data and related data from a variety of sources. The role of the ALE interface is to provide independence between the infrastructure components that acquire the raw EPC data, the architectural components that process the data, and the applications that use the processed data. This document specifies XML schemas, WSDL specifications of WS-I compliant SOAP interfaces, and XML-based bindings for the callback interfaces of the reading and writing APIs. Part 1 of the specification specifies all interfaces that are part of the ALE specification using unified modelling language (UML) notation.
Main sections:

title: Requirements for External Editors of SNOMED CT International Release
description: This user guide describes the knowledge, skills, abilities, education, experience and certification required for individuals external to SNOMED International to author content directly into the SNOMED CT International release.
body:
SNOMED CT is an evolving terminology created to support clinical care, reporting and analysis. As medicine advances, continual updating and maintenance of SNOMED CT is needed to provide the most accurate, current and useful content to clinicians, researchers and data scientists. While SNOMED International has highly trained editors to perform ongoing maintenance, the size and scope of SNOMED CT (over 350,000 active concepts) can cause delays in addressing all the content changes requested by users, as well as in meeting internal requirements to address quality issues identified during routine curation of the terminology. This user guide describes the knowledge, skills, abilities, education, experience and certification required for individuals external to SNOMED International to author content directly into the SNOMED CT International release.

body:
This 1932-page document specifies the set of information object definitions (IODs) of the DICOM standard. IODs are abstract definitions or data models that represent real-world objects. The DICOM IODs relate to communication of digital images and related medical information. For each IOD, this document specifies:

- relationships to associated real-world objects relevant to the IOD
- attributes that describe the characteristics of the IOD.

title: HL7 Cross-Paradigm Specification: Clinical Quality Language, Release 1
description: This specification introduces a high-level, domain-specific language – Clinical Quality Language – focused on clinical quality and targeted at authors of measure and decision support artefacts.
body:
Although there are several standards for expressing clinical quality logic, the standards are not widely adopted and present various barriers to point-to-point sharing of clinical knowledge artefacts. This specification enables shared understanding of clinical knowledge by defining a syntax-independent, canonical representation of logic that can be used to express the knowledge in any given artefact, and enables point-to-point sharing by defining a serialisation for that representation. It introduces a high-level, domain-specific language – Clinical Quality Language – focused on clinical quality and targeted at authors of measure and decision support artefacts.

title: Data Analytics with SNOMED CT
description: This document reviews current approaches, tools and techniques for performing data analytics using SNOMED CT and shares developing practice in this area.
body:
This document reviews current approaches, tools and techniques for performing data analytics using SNOMED CT and shares developing practice in this area.
SNOMED CT is a clinically validated, semantically rich, controlled terminology designed to enable effective representation of clinical information. SNOMED CT is used within electronic health records to support data capture, retrieval, and subsequent reuse for a wide range of purposes. Analytics of this data may be used to describe, predict or improve clinical and business performance, and to recommend action or guide decision making.
SNOMED CT helps to overcome some of the challenges that exist when performing analytics over clinical data, such as the reliability of patient data, terminology/information model boundary issues, concept definition issues and versioning. Using SNOMED CT to support analytics services can help enhance the care of individual patients and populations, and provide cost-effective delivery of care.

SNOMED CT is increasingly being used in clinical decision support (CDS) systems to support healthcare providers in making well informed clinical decisions. title: COVID-19 Data Coding using SNOMED CT
description: This guide provides practical examples of SNOMED CT subsets that can be used to code a variety of COVID-19 related data elements, such as symptoms, risk factors and test results.
body:
As the global terminology for health, SNOMED CT can serve as a common language for recording, sharing, integrating and analysing COVID-19 related data. This guide provides practical examples of SNOMED CT subsets that can be used to code a variety of COVID-19 related data elements, such as symptoms, risk factors and test results. These SNOMED CT subsets can be adapted to meet local requirements for healthcare service delivery, pandemic surveillance, international collaboration and retrospective data analysis. A set of computable SNOMED CT subset artefacts also accompanies this guide.
The primary focus of the guide is to provide example SNOMED CT subsets for data elements covering the following key areas: provider and facility details, patient demographics, clinical assessment, tests and procedures, and treatment and education. These subsets provide examples of SNOMED CT concepts that may be used to code data elements in national reporting requirements, as part of a healthcare data standard (for example, a HL7 FHIR profile or openEHR archetype), or a newly developed information model (data set).

title: SNOMED template syntax specification
description: This specification describes a consistent mechanism for SNOMED CT templates, which can be used to convert any computable SNOMED CT language into a SNOMED CT template language.
body:
This specification describes a consistent mechanism for SNOMED CT templates, which can be used to convert any computable SNOMED CT language into a SNOMED CT template language. It defines the formal rules for representing slots in SNOMED CT expressions, expression constraints or queries. A slot either provides information as to how the template should be processed, or represents a placeholder for a value that is not known at the time of authoring. These placeholders can be completed at a later time using data recorded elsewhere (such as in an information model or entered into a data entry form).
The template syntax may be used in conjunction with any of the computable SNOMED CT languages to represent templates of various kinds. The target audience of this document includes SNOMED International National Release Centres, SNOMED CT designers and developers, and SNOMED CT terminology developers.

title: SNOMED CT Compositional Grammar Specification and Guide v2.4
description: The SNOMED CT Starter Guide is a starting point for learning about SNOMED CT. This guide defines and describes a formal compositional grammar used to represent SNOMED CT expressions as a plain text string.
body:
SNOMED CT is a standard for data elements used in the electronic exchange of clinical information.
SNOMED CT expresses clinical ideas with expressions: structured combinations of one or more concept identifiers. This guide defines and describes a formal compositional grammar used to represent SNOMED CT expressions as a plain text string. This grammar can be both read by humans and computers.
As well as specifying the grammar, this guide presents a logical model for the grammar, discusses example expressions and describes some general implementation considerations.

title: Practical Guide to Reference Sets
description: This user guide provides a high-level introduction to SNOMED CT reference sets and explains the different types of reference sets and their usage. It also introduces the reference set format and provides guidance on the development and management of reference sets.
body:
SNOMED CT provides the core clinical terminology for electronic health records. It contains more than 300,000 active concepts with unique meanings, which are organised into hierarchies and have formal logic-based definitions. When implemented in software applications, SNOMED CT can be used to represent clinically relevant information consistently, reliably and comprehensively. Due to the comprehensiveness and expressivity of SNOMED CT, it is often useful to constrain its use to a subset of concepts, descriptions or relationships relevant to a particular use case. SNOMED CT reference sets provide a standard way to represent subsets of SNOMED CT components. Reference sets also provide an extensible mechanism to customise the terminology to meet a wide range of practical requirements. This user guide provides a high-level introduction to SNOMED CT reference sets and explains the different types of reference sets and their usage. It also introduces the reference set format and provides guidance on the development and management of reference sets.

title: Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise (IHE) Mobile access to Health Documents (MHD) v4.2.3
description: This guideline describes the Mobile access to Health Documents (MHD) Profile, which defines a standardised application programming interface (API) for health document sharing for use by mobile devices.
body:
This guideline describes the Mobile access to Health Documents (MHD) Profile, which defines a standardised application programming interface (API) for health document sharing for use by mobile devices. Mobile devices covered include tablets, smart phones and home-health devices. MHD is intended to make deployment of mobile applications more consistent and reusable. MHD applies to mobile devices that are resource-constrained, have a simple programming environment, simple protocol stack and simple display functionality.
Main sections:
Volume 1: Use-case analysis
- Actors and transactions
- Actor options
- Actor required groupings
- Overview
- Security considerations
- Cross profile considerations
Volume 2: Transaction detail
- Provide Document Bundle [ITI-65]
- Find Document Lists [ITI-66]
- Find Document References [ITI-67]
- Retrieve Document [ITI-68]
- Simplified Publish [ITI-105]
- Generate Metadata [ITI-106]
Volume 3: Metadata
- XDS error codes
- FHIR representation
Other
- Test plan
- Download and analysis

title: HL7 FHIR AU Core Implementation Guide v0.4.0-ci-build
description: This implementation guide describes AU Core, which is provided to support the use of Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) in an Australian context.
body:
This implementation guide describes AU Core, which is provided to support the use of Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) in an Australian context. AU Core defines the data model and RESTful application programming interface (API) interactions that set minimum expectations for a system to record, update, search, and retrieve core digital health and administrative information. AU Core can be implemented as either profile only support or profile support plus interaction support.

body:
This document describes the specifications and use of the GS1 Logistic Label and the Serial Shipping Container Code (SSCC). The GS1 Logistic Label allows users to identify logistic units uniquely so that they can be tracked and traced throughout the supply chain. It is a mandatory requirement that each logistic unit is identified with a unique serial number – the SSCC.

title: Identification of assets – Fact Sheet
description: This document describes the method provided by the GS1 System for identifying assets. The objective of asset identification is to identify a physical entity as an inventory item.
body:
This document describes the method provided by the GS1 System for identifying assets. The objective of asset identification is to identify a physical entity as an inventory item. Companies holding GS1 Company Prefixes may assign asset identifiers to the assets or trade items supplied to their customers. The asset identifier may be a Global Returnable Asset Identifier (GRAI) or a Global Individual Asset Identifier (GIAI).

title: Data structures 024 and 025: Patient Date of Birth and Patient Identification Number, Location Code v2018
subtitle: Table RT018
description: This document is a reference table containing the location codes for use in Patient Date of Birth (ISBT 128 Data Structure 024) and Patient Identification Number (ISBT 128 Data Structure 025).
body: This document is a reference table containing the location codes for use in Patient Date of Birth (ISBT 128 Data Structure 024) and Patient Identification Number (ISBT 128 Data Structure 025). ISBT 128 data structures are formal definitions of how information is to be identified and electronically presented in the transfusion and transplantation information environment. ISBT 128 data structures use a location code which allows the reading system to identify the item from which a code was read (for example, from a wrist band, test tube or clinical notes).

description: This position statement outlines GS1 Healthcare’s position on the implementation of the GS1 DataMatrix, a 2-dimensional barcode symbology.
body: This position statement outlines GS1 Healthcare’s position on the implementation of the GS1 DataMatrix, a 2-dimensional barcode symbology. It sets a goal of year 2015 for the implementation of the GS1 DataMatrix printing on, and scanning of, Regulated Healthcare Trade Items where the current needs are not being met by other GS1 Data Carriers. It also describes how the GS1 DataMatrix meets the needs of pharmaceutical and medical device identification and marking as well as the potential challenges of implementation.

description: This document provides a mechanism for the transport of DICOM metadata associated with a video or an audio flow based on the SMPTE ST 2110-20 and SMPTE ST 2110-30, respectively.
body:
This document, part of the Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) standard, specifies an SMPTE ST 2110-10 based service, relying on RTP, for the real-time transport of DICOM metadata. It provides a mechanism for the transport of DICOM metadata associated with a video or an audio flow based on the SMPTE ST 2110-20 and SMPTE ST 2110-30, respectively.
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.

body:

The document works as an implementation guide for CDA R2, focusing on the use and reference of data collected in imaging procedures as explicit evidence within reports.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: National Electrical Manufacturers Association Standard PS3: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) Part 3.19 v2024b
subtitle: DICOM PS3.19 Application hosting
description: This document defines an Application Program Interface (API) between the hosting system and hosted application.
body:
This document defines an Application Program Interface (API) between 2 software applications:
- the hosting system, which provides the second application with data (such as a set of images and related data)
- the hosted application, which analyses the data and may return results to the first application. 
The API standardises the data interchange between software components on the same system so that hosted application programs can plug into hosting systems. 
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.
Main sections:
- Application hosting overview
- Hosted application life cycle
- Interfaces
- Data types and structures
- Data exchange model conventions

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: National Electrical Manufacturers Association Standard PS3: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) Part 3.15 v2024b
subtitle: DICOM PS3.15 Security and system management profiles
description: This document specifies security and system management profiles to which implementations may claim conformance.
body:
This document specifies security and system management profiles to which implementations may claim conformance. Security and system management profiles are defined by reference to external protocols (such as TLS, ISCL, DHCP, and LDAP) used in a system that uses DICOM standard protocols for information interchange. The system management profiles specified support automation of the configuration management processes needed to operate a system that uses DICOM standard protocols. The document does not address security policies, but provides mechanisms that could be used to implement security policies on the interchange of DICOM objects between Application Entities.
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard. 
Main sections:
- Security and system management profile outlines
- Configuration profiles

title: National Electrical Manufacturers Association Standard PS3: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) Part 3.14
subtitle: DICOM PS3.14 Grayscale standard display function
description: This document specifies a standardised display function for the display of greyscale images.
body:
This document specifies a standardised display function for the display of greyscale images. It describes how to measure the characteristic curve of a particular display system to either:
- change the display system (such as a monitor or printer) to match the greyscale standard display function
- measure the conformance of a display system to the greyscale standard display function. 
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.
Main sections:
- The greyscale standard display function

title: National Electrical Manufacturers Association Standard PS3: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) Part 3.10 v2024b
subtitle: DICOM PS3.10 Media storage and file format for media interchange
description: This document specifies a general model for the storage of medical imaging information on removable media.
body:
This document, part of the DICOM standard, specifies a general model for the storage of medical imaging information on removable media. It provides a framework allowing the interchange of various types of medical images and related information on a broad range of physical storage media. 
This document specifies:
- a layered model for storing medical images and related information
- a DICOM file format
- a DICOM secure file format
- a DICOM file service.
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: National Electrical Manufacturers Association Standard PS3: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) Part 3.7 v2024b
subtitle: DICOM PS3.7 Message exchange
description: This document specifies the DICOM Message Service Element (DIMSE).
body:
This document is part of the DICOM standard and specifies the DICOM Message Service Element (DIMSE). 
The DIMSE defines an Application Service Element (both the service and protocol) used by peer DICOM Application Entities for the purpose of exchanging medical images and related information. To do this, it relies on the DIMSE protocol, which defines the encoding rules necessary to construct Messages. A Message is composed of a Command Set (defined in this Part of the DICOM Standard) followed by a conditional Data Set (defined in PS3.5).
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.
Main sections:
- Service context
- Service overview
- Protocol overview
- DIMSE-C
- DIMSE-N

subtitle: DICOM PS3.5 Data structure and encoding
description: This document specifies the structure and encoding of the DICOM message data set. The data set is the part of the DICOM message that conveys information about real-world objects managed over a network.
body:
This 158-page document specifies the structure and encoding of the DICOM message data set. The data set is the part of the DICOM message that conveys information about real-world objects managed over a network. This document specifies:
- the encoding of values
- the structure and usage of a data set
- the usage of a data element and relationships to other data elements
- the construction and usage of nested data sets
- the construction and usage of data sets containing pixel data
- how to uniquely identify information
- the specification of the standard DICOM transfer syntax.
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: National Electrical Manufacturers Association Standard PS3: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) Part 3.4 v2024b
subtitle: DICOM PS3.4 Service class specifications
description: This document specifies the set of service class definitions of the DICOM standard.
body:
This 482-page document specifies the set of service class definitions of the DICOM standard. Service class definitions are abstract definitions of real-world activities applicable to communication of digital medical information. For each service class definition, this document specifies:
- the semantic description of the activities of the Service Class Definition
- the group of DIMSE Service operations and notifications applicable to the Service Class Description
- one or more functionally-related Service-Object Pair (SOP) Classes that are supported by the Service Class Definition and may be performed between peer DICOM Application Entities
- the relationship of each SOP class to applicable Information Object Definitions specified in PS3.3 Information object definitions.
This document is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.
Main sections:
- Scope and field of application
- Normative references
- Definitions
- Symbols and abbreviations
- Conventions
- DICOM information model
- DICOM model of the real world
- Coded entry usage in service specifications
- 37 service classes and SOP classes

organisation: Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM)
title: DICOM PS3.2 2024b - Conformance
description: This standard specifies the minimum general conformance requirements that must be met by any implementation claiming conformance to the DICOM Standard, the purpose and structure of a Conformance Statement, and how conformance information can be placed into a Conformance Statement.
body:
Conformance Statements are critical to interoperability. They help implementers and system integrators determine whether or not applications do interoperate. Information in a conformance statement can also be used to resolve problems. 
This standard specifies:
- minimum general conformance requirements that must be met by any implementation claiming conformance to the DICOM Standard
- the purpose and structure of a Conformance Statement
- how conformance information can be placed into a Conformance Statement.
Main sections:
- Conventions
- Purpose of a conformance statement
- Conformance requirements

subtitle: DICOM PS3.1 Introduction and overview
description: This document provides an overview of the DICOM standard that supports interoperability of medical imaging information.
body:
This document provides an overview of the DICOM standard, including its history, scope, goals and structure. DICOM supports interoperability of medical imaging information. It is a free international standard for the transmittal, storage, retrieval, printing, processing and displaying of medical imaging information between imaging equipment and other systems. 
This overview is one of 22 parts to the DICOM standard.
Main sections:
- Scope and field of application
- Normative references
- Definitions
- Symbols and abbreviations
- The DICOM communication model
- Overview of the content of the DICOM standard
- Referencing the DICOM standard
